barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Double Initial Procedure Program
Role: Prevent wrong material addition to batch
LOD Basis: Procedural control requiring two employees to verify chemical identity and initial batch ticket before charging
supporting_text: ["AB Specialty developed a double initial procedure practice in 2014 in an effort to prevent employees from charging the wrong materials to batch processes", "The occurrence of the May 3 incident indicates that AB Specialty's double initial procedure program did not prevent a wrong material from being added to the tank", "Operator 1 likely misidentified the 10% KOH drum(s) that remained in the emulsions area after the first batch for XL 10 and added 10% KOH into the tank"]
incident_summary: On May 3, 2019, at AB Specialty Silicones in Waukegan, Illinois, an operator pumped an incorrect chemical (10% KOH) into a batch tank containing XL 10 and TD 6/12 Blend. The incompatible chemicals were stored in nearly identical 55-gallon blue drums, differing only in small labels. The reaction between XL 10 (containing silicon hydride bonds) and aqueous KOH produced hydrogen gas, which released into the production building. The hydrogen gas ignited, causing a massive explosion and fire that fatally injured four employees (Byron Biehn, Jeffrey Cummings, Daniel Nicklas, Allen Stevens), destroyed the production building, and forced operational disruptions.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Pressure test procedure per 30 CFR 250.1715
Role: Detect failed cement squeeze and valve leakage
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["The pressure test failed to hold 1000 psi over 15 minutes as required per 30 CFR 250.1715"]
incident_summary: On 18 March 2022 at 1800 hours, during abandonment operations on Shell's Alaminos Canyon 815 well SA001 from the Helix Q-4000, a pressure test of a five-barrel cement squeeze failed to hold 1000 psi. Investigation revealed the chemical injection tree valve 1 (CIT1) on the subsea tree was leaking approximately 188 barrels of a 50/50 mixture of Mono Ethylene Glycol and seawater into the Gulf of Mexico. The CIT1 valve had not been functioned to verify closure prior to the pressure test; operators relied on documented position from well handover procedures. Manual ROV intervention and hydraulic functioning of the valve eventually stopped the leak.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Personal H2S Detection Device
Role: Detection of H2S presence to alert worker
LOD Basis: Personal protective equipment to detect hazardous gas exposure
supporting_text: ["Pumper A was not wearing his personal H2S detection device upon entering the waterflood station on the night of the incident", "there is no evidence that Aghorn management required the use of these devices"]
incident_summary: On October 26, 2019, Pumper A responded to a pump oil level alarm at Aghorn's Foster D waterflood station. While attempting to isolate Pump #1 without performing Lockout/Tagout, the pump automatically energized, causing a broken plunger to release water containing hydrogen sulfide gas. Pumper A was fatally injured from H2S exposure. Subsequently, Pumper A's spouse gained access to the unlocked facility, was also exposed to H2S, and was fatally injured. The incident was attributed to multiple safety failures including nonuse of personal H2S detectors, lack of Lockout/Tagout procedures, inadequate ventilation, nonfunctioning H2S detection and alarm systems, and deficient site security.

incident_summary: On October 26, 2019, Pumper A responded to a pump oil level alarm at Aghorn's Foster D waterflood station. While attempting to isolate Pump #1 without performing Lockout/Tagout, the pump automatically energized, causing a broken plunger to release water containing hydrogen sulfide gas. Pumper A was fatally injured from H2S exposure. Subsequently, Pumper A's spouse gained access to the unlocked facility, was also exposed to H2S, and was fatally injured. The incident was attributed to multiple safety failures including nonuse of personal H2S detectors, lack of Lockout/Tagout procedures, inadequate ventilation, nonfunctioning H2S detection and alarm systems, and deficient site security.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Process Hazard Analysis methodology
Role: Identify and evaluate process safety hazards
LOD Basis: Systematic identification of hazards in nitrous oxide operations
supporting_text: ["Airgas developed and implemented recommended PSM components for its nitrous oxide business by creating a new Industrial Risk Management Program, a process hazard analysis methodology"]
incident_summary: On August 28, 2016, a nitrous oxide trailer truck exploded at the Airgas manufacturing facility in Cantonment, Florida, fatally injuring one Airgas employee and heavily damaging the facility. The most probable cause was that during initial loading of a trailer truck, a pump heated nitrous oxide above safe operating limits, likely starting a nitrous oxide decomposition reaction that propagated from the pump into the trailer truck, causing the explosion. The CSB determined that Airgas lacked an effective safety management system to identify, evaluate, and control process safety-related hazards. Following the incident, Airgas rapidly implemented comprehensive process safety management components for its nitrous oxide operations.

incident_summary: On August 31, 2017, organic peroxides stored in refrigerated trailers at the Arkema Crosby facility decomposed and ignited following catastrophic flooding from Hurricane Harvey. Extreme winds tore off portions of the roof of Plant 4, which stored approximately 70,000-100,000 pounds of TCCA-based formulation. Rainwater from the hurricane contacted the stored TCCA-based formulation, initiating a chemical reaction and decomposition that released toxic chlorine gas and caused a fire. The decomposition and fire destroyed Plant 4 and damaged additional structures. A massive plume of hazardous gases crossed the facility boundary and was visible over a large portion of the nearby community. Interstate 10 was closed for over 28 hours, and a shelter-in-place order was issued. No injuries were reported. Approximately $250 million was invested in redesigning and reconstructing the facility, which resumed operations around March 2023.

incident_summary: On September 20, 2022, at approximately 6:46 p.m., a vapor cloud ignited causing a flash fire at the BP-Husky Toledo Refinery in Oregon, Ohio. Two BP employees (brothers Ben and Max Morrissey) were fatally injured when they released approximately 23,000 pounds of flammable liquid naphtha from a pressurized Fuel Gas Mix Drum to the ground. incident_summary: On 6 June 2022 at 1030 hours, a subsea leak of approximately 9 barrels of hydrocarbon was discovered at Well TD004 (Slot 47/MC822-13/TD004) on the Thunderhorse platform in Mississippi Canyon Block 778. The leak was caused by a rod piston seal failure in the Surface Controlled Subsurface Safety Valve (SCSSV). The incident was preceded by a platform power loss on 5 June 2022 that triggered an unplanned shut-in and revealed increased hydraulic fluid consumption. The leak was detected through ROV inspection and resulted in a visible sheen observed in satellite imagery and during aerial surveys. BP's subsea leak detection system (C-ROC Algorithm) was active but could not detect this relatively small discharge.

incident_summary: On 8 June 2022 at 1310 hours, production personnel at MC 778 Thunderhorse South reported an external subsea leak at the P2 Riser Touchdown Connector - Pipeline End Termination (PLET) No. 5. incident_summary: On October 23, 2009, at approximately 12:23 a.m., a 5-million gallon aboveground storage tank (Tank 409) at the CAPECO facility in Bayamón, Puerto Rico overflowed during offloading of gasoline from the tanker ship Cape Bruny. Approximately 194,000 gallons of gasoline escaped, forming a large vapor cloud that encompassed 107 acres. The vapor cloud migrated through open dike drain valves to low-lying areas and the wastewater treatment area, where it ignited. The resulting explosion registered 2.9 on the Richter scale, damaged approximately 300 homes and businesses up to 1.25 miles away, caused over $5 million in damages to Fort Buchanan military facility, and resulted in fires that burned for nearly 60 hours, destroying or damaging 17 of 48 petroleum storage tanks. Environmental contamination included petroleum products leaking into soil, nearby wetlands, and navigable waterways.

incident_summary: On October 23, 2009, at approximately 12:23 a.m., a 5-million gallon aboveground storage tank (Tank 409) at the CAPECO facility in Bayamón, Puerto Rico overflowed during offloading of gasoline from the tanker ship Cape Bruny. Approximately 194,000 gallons of gasoline escaped, forming a large vapor cloud that encompassed 107 acres. The vapor cloud migrated through open dike drain valves to low-lying areas and the wastewater treatment area, where it ignited. The resulting explosion registered 2.9 on the Richter scale, damaged approximately 300 homes and businesses up to 1.25 miles away, caused over $5 million in damages to Fort Buchanan military facility, and resulted in fires that burned for nearly 60 hours, destroying or damaging 17 of 48 petroleum storage tanks. Environmental contamination included petroleum products leaking into soil, nearby wetlands, and navigable waterways.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Sulfidation Corrosion Inspection Program
Role: Detection of corrosion damage
LOD Basis: Inspection and monitoring to detect wall thinning before failure
supporting_text: ["Inspection data for the 4-sidecut piping, where measurements were historically taken on high-silicon fittings, indicated the 4-sidecut piping could safely operate through 2016", "A Crude Unit metallurgical analysis recommendation to perform 100 percent volumetric inspection of the 4-sidecut line submitted for the 2007 turnaround was approved", "the guided wave bracelets were only installed on a small portion of the 4-sidecut line which did not include the 52-inch component that ultimately failed"]
incident_summary: On August 6, 2012, a catastrophic rupture occurred in the 4-sidecut piping of the #4 Crude Unit at Chevron Richmond Refinery. The 52-inch component of the 8-inch line ruptured due to sulfidation corrosion, releasing approximately 10,800 barrels per day of light gas oil. The release vaporized into a large vapor cloud that engulfed 19 employees. Approximately two minutes after release, the vapor ignited, resulting in a large fire. One firefighter was caught in the fireball but escaped due to protective equipment. Six employees suffered minor injuries. Approximately 15,000 people from surrounding communities sought medical treatment. The incident resulted in a Community Warning System Level 3 alert and shelter-in-place advisory for Richmond, San Pablo, and North Richmond.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Back Pressure Valve (BPV) for NBK 0420 Chemelectric Oil Treater
Role: Prevent overpressurization of treater by maintaining system back pressure
LOD Basis: Primary pressure control device on treater inlet
supporting_text: ["the back pressure valve (BPV) for the NBK 0420 Chemelectric Oil Treater (Treater) failed to operate as designed", "The Treater's back pressure valve remained open sending 94 PSI of gas into the treater"]
incident_summary: On 19-OCT-2022, during facility startup after a three-day shutdown, the back pressure valve (BPV) for the NBK 0420 Chemelectric Oil Treater failed to operate as designed. The valve remained open, allowing 94 PSI of gas into the treater, causing the Pressure Safety Valve to relieve to the relief header and vent flare. The vent flare emitted a fine mist of oily water to the atmosphere. An estimated 0.774 gallons of oil entered Gulf of Mexico waters, with satellite imagery later detecting a sheen measuring 2.44 nautical miles in length and 2.67 nautical miles at the widest point. The incident was reported to the National Response Center.

incident_summary: On 27 January 2022 at approximately 1625 hours, a fire occurred near the starter of a rental gas compressor on Main Pass 73-A production platform. The fire resulted from an improperly tightened JIC fitting on the engine's starter exhaust hose that backed off over time due to vibration, combined with a leaking Flow Safety Valve (FSV) that allowed natural gas to migrate to the hot exhaust area (>900 degrees). Four platform operators successfully extinguished the fire using handheld fire extinguishers within three minutes. No injuries or pollution occurred. Damage was limited to compressor wiring and FSV internal parts, estimated at $12,000.

incident_summary: On 25 February 2022 at 0530 hours, a pollution event occurred at West Delta 73 C production platform. An estimated 660 gallons (later revised to approximately 111 gallons) of unrecoverable oil was discharged into the Gulf of Mexico through the platform's vent boom. The incident resulted from a combination of a Water Skimmer float device failure (pinhole defect) and failure of the Level Safety High (LSH) device on the Vent Scrubber to operate as designed. Contributing factor was operator fatigue, with OP2 working 21 hours (0600 on 24 Feb to 0300 on 25 Feb) and becoming extremely fatigued before the incident occurred.

incident_summary: This CSB Safety Study examines six major incidents (1975-2019) where lack of effective remote isolation equipment contributed to escalation of loss-of-containment events, resulting in fatalities, serious injuries, environmental contamination, and massive property damage. The study concludes that regulatory requirements for remote isolation of process equipment are necessary to prevent future incidents.

incident_summary: On July 31, 2024, at approximately 8:20 p.m., an emergency pressure relief valve on Heat Exchanger 5 Surge Drum in Tank Farm 5 opened due to overpressure, discharging approximately 275 pounds of anhydrous ammonia to the atmosphere. The discharged ammonia contained a high level of liquid, causing it to rapidly drop to ground level near the employee parking lot and building. During evacuation, 33 workers were injured by ammonia inhalation, with 4 admitted to hospitals. The incident was caused by overpressure in the vessel, failure to discharge to a safe location, and insufficient emergency preparedness including lack of effective alarms and emergency shutdown procedures.

incident_summary: On April 30, 2018, Murphy reported a subsea umbilical leak from a chemical injection line (Hydraulic Flying Lead) for the De Soto Canyon 4 subsea well. The leak was discovered by ROV during a subsea survey. Two leaks were identified: one on the HFL between the manifold and SUTA, and another on the pressure cap of the PLET. The chemical discharged was paraffin inhibitor containing naphthalene. The outer jacket of the hose was damaged due to chafing against the mounting bracket, compromising collapse resistance. Differential pressure across the inner sheath went negative, causing collapse and tearing of the internal pressure sheath. Maximum estimated volume discharged was 900 bbls, though BSEE calculated worst case discharge at 37,000 to 1.6 million gallons based on May 2, 2017 start date.

incident_summary: On April 30, 2018, Murphy reported a subsea umbilical leak from a chemical injection line (Hydraulic Flying Lead) for the De Soto Canyon 4 subsea well. The leak was discovered by ROV during a subsea survey. Two leaks were identified: one on the HFL between the manifold and SUTA, and another on the pressure cap of the PLET. The chemical discharged was paraffin inhibitor containing naphthalene. The outer jacket of the hose was damaged due to chafing against the mounting bracket, compromising collapse resistance. Differential pressure across the inner sheath went negative, causing collapse and tearing of the internal pressure sheath. Maximum estimated volume discharged was 900 bbls, though BSEE calculated worst case discharge at 37,000 to 1.6 million gallons based on May 2, 2017 start date.

barrier_role_match_text:

Role: Detection of gas presence before explosion

incident_summary: On 24 June 2022 at approximately 1147 hours, two Gulf Island Services (GIS) employees were performing construction work on Main Pass 260 Platform P, a right-of-way platform owned by Destin Pipeline Company located 55 miles offshore in 300 feet of water. While installing a skillet to isolate the flare scrubber and flare, workers unbolted a 10-inch flange and determined the skillet did not fit. While waiting for the correct size skillet, a handheld gas detector alarmed, pressure was heard escaping from the flange, and an explosion occurred with a large plume of smoke. Both workers reported burning sensations and smoke inhalation. Both were transported to an onshore medical facility and released the same day without restrictions. No pollution or facility damage occurred. BSEE investigation identified three plausible causes: pyrophoric iron (FeS) combustion when exposed to oxygen through the opened flange (most likely), galvanic cell corrosion from a chain hoist generating electric current, or hydrate release within the piping.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Process Hazard Recognition and Assessment
Role: Identify combustible dust hazards in process streams
LOD Basis: NFPA guidance on hazard identification
supporting_text: ["Didion did not accurately assess a number of process streams in the mill buildings that contained combustible dust hazards", "The failure to recognize that interconnecting equipment through dust collectors could present a deflagration propagation hazard", "The incorrect conclusion, using incorrect calculations, that the dust collectors in the mill facility did not contain explosive dust concentrations"]
incident_summary: On May 31, 2017, at approximately 11:00 p.m., multiple combustible dust explosions occurred at the Didion Milling dry corn milling facility in Cambria, Wisconsin. The incident fatally injured five employees and injured 14 others. During the day of the incident, Didion connected the discharge of two rotary gap mills to increase production. On the night of the incident, employees smelled smoke and investigated the source. A fire spread through process equipment via interconnected dust collection systems, resulting in explosions in dust collection equipment. Secondary explosions occurred throughout the facility, causing the collapse of multiple buildings including the Multipurpose Building, F Mill, and the Boiler House. The estimated property damage was $15,375,959 with no off-site consequences.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Pressure Safety Valve on MBF-V940 Fuel Gas Scrubber
Role: Prevent overpressurization upstream of fuel gas filter
LOD Basis: Primary pressure relief device upstream of MAK-F960A
supporting_text: ["The Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) on the MBF-V940 Fuel Gas Scrubber was set for 270 PSI, it did not relieve until 390 PSI.", "During testing of safety devices the PSV on MBF-V940 was set to relieve at 270 PSI; it did not relieve until 390 PSI, thus allowing 390 PSI or greater pressure to the MAK-F960A Fuel Gas Filter."]
incident_summary: On 9 May 2015 at approximately 1728 hours, the MAK-F960A Fuel Gas Filter vessel experienced catastrophic overpressurization during facility restart following a pressure upset. The vessel top was blown off, struck a nearby handrail creating sparks that ignited streaming fuel gas. The incident resulted in a fire that was extinguished by operators using portable fire extinguishers. Root causes included severe corrosion of vessel mounting tabs, failed pressure safety valves both upstream and downstream of the filter, unauthorized modifications to the filter vessels that removed differential gauges and altered level indicators, and a single-stage pressure reduction system that was inadequate for the operating conditions.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Depressurization of vessel prior to component removal
Role: Critical mitigation to prevent pressurized release
LOD Basis: Primary control to eliminate pressure hazard before opening vessel
supporting_text: ["the vessel was not depressurized and the hot oil was not drained to a safe level below the TC", "critical mitigations were not executed prior to removing the TC from the vessel"]
incident_summary: On June 23, 2014, at approximately 10:30 A.M., the Person in Charge (PIC) at Arena Offshore East Cameron 328 B facility was replacing a faulty pneumatic temperature controller (TC) on Heater Treater #2 (NBK-2770). The TC was installed directly into a thread-o-let within the liquid medium of the vessel instead of inside a thermo-well as recommended by the manufacturer. Prior to removal, the vessel was not depressurized and hot oil was not drained to a safe level. When the TC was removed, hot pressurized oil blew out of the one inch opening, striking and burning the PIC on his left bicep. Oil continued to flow across the deck, striking the line heater approximately 20 feet away. Operations personnel manually shut down the facility and installed a nipple and ball valve to isolate the leak. A total of 12 gallons of crude oil was discharged into offshore waters.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: BAHX design and material selection
Role: Inherent design to minimize thermal stress concentration
LOD Basis: Equipment design and selection
supporting_text: ["The probable cause of this incident was the failure of a brazed aluminum heat exchanger (BAHX) due to thermal fatigue.", "Thermal fatigue, as a damage mechanism, commonly forms small cracks in BAHXs."]
incident_summary: On June 27, 2016, at approximately 11:22 p.m., a brazed aluminum heat exchanger (BAHX) on the A-Train process line failed due to thermal fatigue, resulting in a major loss of containment of flammable hydrocarbons. The released hydrocarbons ignited, initiating a series of fires and explosions that caused 13 different ruptures of piping and equipment over approximately 35 minutes. Two workers on the night shift were uninjured. The facility experienced extensive damage and was shut down for almost six months. Enterprise incurred $10.4 million in fire response expenses and a non-cash loss of $7.1 million in damaged assets. A total of 104,000 pounds of emissions were released, with most burned off by fires and flares.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Pressure relief devices (Rupture Discs)
Role: Prevent overpressurization by venting excess pressure
LOD Basis: Primary safety device to protect against overpressure
supporting_text: ["the relief valve installed on the bottle during a preventative maintenance program in December 2023 was rated for 9,000 psi instead of the appropriate 2,400 psi", "Had the cylinders been fitted with the correct relief valve, the over-pressurization could have been avoided", "the installation of a relief device with a burst pressure greater than the cylinder's design pressure contributed to the failure"]
incident_summary: On April 28, 2025, at approximately 10:30 a.m., a nitrogen bottle ruptured and exploded during refilling operations on the BW Pioneer FPSO in Walker Ridge Block 249. The bottle had been mistakenly assumed to be rated for 6,000 psi when it was only rated for 2,400 psi, and was recharged to more than double its maximum capacity. Additionally, an incorrect relief valve rated for 9,000 psi had been installed instead of the appropriate 2,400 psi valve. The explosion caused moderate to severe structural damage to the bottle rack, manifold, diesel fuel tank, and nearby equipment. A crane operator located approximately 18 feet away was struck by flying debris, sustaining minor bruises, a small neck lesion, and temporary hearing impairment. The root cause was over-pressurization due to equipment failure (incorrect relief valve) combined with human error (failure to identify correct pressure rating) and inadequate procedures and training.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Wellhead coating maintenance program
Role: Prevent corrosion of alignment pin plug
LOD Basis: Preventive maintenance to protect equipment from corrosive environment
supporting_text: ["The failure of the alignment pin plug is likely due to corrosion. This corrosion was caused by a failure of the coating. The coating was not properly maintained by Fieldwood.", "Fieldwood's failure to maintain proper coatings against the saltwater laden environment led to excessive corrosion and subsequent failure of the alignment pin plug."]
incident_summary: On 10 January 2022, a well control and pollution event occurred at WD 80 D production platform in the Gulf of Mexico. The incident originated from the D-36 well where an alignment pin plug on the tubing head had been leaking gas since 11 January 2021. During a coiled tubing operation to set a plug and clean scale, the tool became stuck downhole and required mechanical extraction, which exacerbated tubing-to-casing communication. This caused the A Annulus pressure to rise from 50 psi to 2000 psi, resulting in excessive fluid returns to the wet oil tank. Approximately 107 gallons (2.5 barrels) of hydrocarbons were spilled. The platform was evacuated and remediation efforts continued through March 2022. Root causes included equipment failure (packer assembly design flaw, alignment pin corrosion due to inadequate coating maintenance), human performance error during coiled tubing operations, and organizational failures in communication and urgency to remediate.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Routine Corrosion Assessment and Maintenance Program
Role: Prevent piping corrosion and loss of containment
LOD Basis: Preventive maintenance is the primary defense against corrosion-induced piping failure
supporting_text: ["Failure of the Operator to perform routine corrosion assessment maintenance on piping was the primary cause of this incident", "The condition of the piping showed evidence of a lack of routine maintenance", "The corrosion that allowed the H2S to leak was a direct result of a lack of routine piping maintenance on the facility"]
incident_summary: On 29-May-2016 at 1310 hrs, a hydrogen sulfide (H2S) gas release of 400 ppm occurred on Main Pass 299-FP Platform during pigging operations on the flowline from MP 299-A to FP Platform. While the crew drained fluids from the pig trap, H2S sensors detected high concentrations of sour gas. The H2S alarm sounded and the platform was shut in. All personnel donned SCBAs and mustered. After an initial all-clear declaration, the H2S alarm sounded again. The response team located a gas leak in the 2-inch pipe outlet from the MBF-935 Buyback Gas Scrubber below the south side cellar deck. Investigation determined the leak was caused by extensive exterior corrosion that compromised the piping integrity. The root cause was identified as failure of the operator to perform routine corrosion assessment and maintenance on piping. The field was subsequently shut in indefinitely as continued operation was deemed uneconomical.

After an initial all-clear declaration, the H2S alarm sounded again. The response team located a gas leak in the 2-inch pipe outlet from the MBF-935 Buyback Gas Scrubber below the south side cellar deck. Investigation determined the leak was caused by extensive exterior corrosion that compromised the piping integrity. The root cause was identified as failure of the operator to perform routine corrosion assessment and maintenance on piping. The field was subsequently shut in indefinitely as continued operation was deemed uneconomical.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Bubble-type level gauge design and redundancy
Role: Primary liquid level measurement and control device
LOD Basis: Critical component for preventing overflow
supporting_text: ["The bubble-type level gauge probably bent during maintenance activity, preventing it from measuring and controlling the liquid nitrogen level in the freezer", "The design was vulnerable to a single point of failure. Once the gauge bent, there was nothing else to prevent the liquid nitrogen from leaking", "During manufacturing of the immersion freezer, Messer failed to detect a manufacturing defect that exacerbated the likelihood of the bubble-type level gauge bending: a clamp intended to secure the bubble-type level gauge to the freezer was missing"]
incident_summary: On January 28, 2021, liquid nitrogen overflowed from an immersion freezer at Foundation Food Group's Plant 4 in Gainesville, Georgia. The overflow occurred while maintenance workers were troubleshooting operational issues with the freezer, which was designed and owned by Messer LLC and rented to FFG. Once released, the liquid nitrogen vaporized, expanded, and rapidly accumulated within a partially enclosed cold room lacking mechanical ventilation. Two maintenance workers died from asphyxiation due to vaporized nitrogen. The uncontrolled nitrogen leak continued undetected for 30-60 minutes. When discovered, at least 14 FFG employees responded by entering the cold room or attempting rescue, resulting in four additional fatalities and three employees plus one firefighter suffering severe asphyxiation symptoms. The incident was caused by failure of the liquid level control system due to deformation of the bubble-type level gauge component, combined with inadequate process safety management, emergency preparedness, atmospheric monitoring, and product stewardship practices.

incident_summary: On January 9, 2014, approximately 11,000 gallons of a mixture containing Crude MCHM and PPH, stripped leaked from tank 396 at Freedom Industries facility in Charleston, West Virginia. The leak resulted from pitting corrosion that created two holes (0.75 and 0.4 inches diameter) in the tank floor. The chemical mixture escaped through failed secondary containment and an underground culvert, flowing into the Elk River approximately 1.5 miles upstream of the West Virginia American Water treatment facility intake. The contamination resulted in a Do Not Use order affecting approximately 300,000 residents across nine counties. Over 300 people sought medical treatment for symptoms including nausea, rashes, vomiting, abdominal pain, and diarrhea. The incident exposed significant gaps in tank inspection, secondary containment maintenance, leak detection systems, source water protection, and public health communication.

incident_summary: On November 8, 2017 at 1:18 a.m., an explosion occurred at the 30-inch sales gas pipeline pig launcher on the Enchilada platform (GB Block 128) operated by Shell Offshore Inc. Two operators were preparing to launch a pig when the pig launcher closure door failed explosively, propelling the door and other items overboard. A fire ensued, fed by natural gas from the pipeline. One operator sustained concussion, back injuries, and second-degree burns; the other suffered minor injuries. All 46 platform personnel were safely evacuated. The root cause was incomplete closure of the clamp rings due to procedural failures in securing the closure door, combined with inadequate hazard analysis, improper management of change, and insufficient supervision.

incident_summary: At approximately 23:00 hours on 23 May 2014, the Diamond Ocean Onyx semi-submersible drilling rig reported a loss of synthetic base mud (SBM) during abandonment operations on Well #1. Initial report of 107 bbl was revised to 61 bbl after strapping calculations. The spill occurred when mud line valves on the Schlumberger Cement Unit were left in the open position after cleaning operations and were not checked prior to the cement job. When the Cementer began pumping cement from Batch Mixing Tank #2, the tank overflowed, causing SBM to spill into secondary containment areas and then into drains leading to the overboard discharge line, which also had open valves. The Toolpusher closed the overboard drain line valves at 23:30 hours, preventing further discharge. No injuries or property damage occurred.

incident_summary: On 7 June 2017, the Noble Don Taylor drillship under contract to Shell reported an unauthorized discharge of approximately 94.2 barrels of 15.1 ppg synthetic oil base mud during exploration operations on Well DC005 (Cardamom) in Garden Banks Block 427, Gulf of Mexico. The spill was caused by failure of the upper variable bore ram (VBR) aft bonnet door cartridge seal on the subsea blowout preventer. incident_summary: On 23 March 2015 at 0505 hours, the Rowan Reliance drill ship under contract to Cobalt International Energy reported a discharge of approximately 2200 barrels of 15.3 ppg synthetic oil base mud (SOBM) during well operations on Well 002 (North Platte #2) in Garden Banks Block 958 at 4846 ft water depth. The primary cause was the failure to install a Seal Sub (Mud Pin) in the flange connection between the flex joint termination spool and the first riser joint (Joint #64). Contributing factors included inadequate procedures, insufficient crew handover, failure to recognize the SOBM buildup during ROV coverage, command structure issues, and under-torqued bolts (1250 psi instead of required 1600 psi). Of the 2200 bbl lost, 1557 bbl entered open water and 643 bbl were lost in open hole. Property damage totaled $151,353.

incident_summary: On 23 March 2015 at 0505 hours, the Rowan Reliance drill ship under contract to Cobalt International Energy reported a discharge of approximately 2200 barrels of 15.3 ppg synthetic oil base mud (SOBM) during well operations on Well 002 (North Platte #2) in Garden Banks Block 958 at 4846 ft water depth. The primary cause was the failure to install a Seal Sub (Mud Pin) in the flange connection between the flex joint termination spool and the first riser joint (Joint #64). Contributing factors included inadequate procedures, insufficient crew handover, failure to recognize the SOBM buildup during ROV coverage, command structure issues, and under-torqued bolts (1250 psi instead of required 1600 psi). Of the 2200 bbl lost, 1557 bbl entered open water and 643 bbl were lost in open hole. Property damage totaled $151,353.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Mud gas separator with liquid leg
Role: Separate hydrocarbon gas from return fluids
LOD Basis: Primary engineering control to prevent gas entering shaker house

incident_summary: On 15 November 2024 at 0837 hours, a gas release occurred in the shaker house aboard the Valaris DS-16 drillship during well abandonment operations. While displacing the kill line and landing string back to calcium bromide brine after nitrogen displacement, hydrocarbons were released into the shaker house atmosphere. The incident was triggered by a compromised liquid leg in the mud gas separator, allowing hydrocarbon gas to bypass the separator and enter the shaker house. A hydrocarbon alarm activated, prompting evacuation and crew muster. Gas levels returned to zero and operations resumed without further issues.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Chemical Injection Quills Installation
Role: Prevent concentrated chemical attack on pipe wall
LOD Basis: Primary engineering control to ensure proper chemical distribution
supporting_text: ["no quills were installed into the fittings to enable the injected chemicals to be released midstream into the pipe", "By injecting the chemicals into the piping without the use of the quills, the chemicals concentrated on the interior pipe wall and down the length of the pipe, causing severe deterioration and metal loss"]
incident_summary: On 16-OCT-2015 at 2015 hours, an 18-inch LP pipe ruptured on the Front Runner spar at GC 338A, releasing approximately 25 barrels of oil and undetermined amount of gas. The rupture occurred at the chemical injection point where the line penetrated the production deck. Root cause was severe internal corrosion (metal loss up to 50.4%) caused by acidic chemical injection without proper distribution via quills. Contributing factors included failure to use quills for chemical distribution and inadequate NDT testing that did not cover injection point areas. One employee was injured. Total estimated damage: $1.5 million.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Dynamic Positioning System
Role: Maintain rig position during adverse weather
LOD Basis: Primary control to prevent rig drift
supporting_text: ["the drillship's DP position was compromised into a red alert status due to high winds and a misaligned thruster", "Thruster-2 is located on the port bow and was found to be 180 degrees out of alignment", "When the DP system increased the thrust on Thruster-2, it increased but in the opposite direction, thus going in the same direction as the wind"]
incident_summary: On January 15, 2022, at approximately 16:10 hours, the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse experienced a Dynamic Position (DP) system failure due to misalignment of Thruster-2 (180 degrees out of alignment) during completion operations at Green Canyon Block 389. High winds and sudden wind direction changes caused the misaligned thruster to push the drillship in the same direction as the wind, compromising DP position into red alert status. The Emergency Disconnect Sequence was initiated, unlatching the Lower Marine Riser Package (LMRP) from the Blow Out Preventers (BOP). This resulted in an unauthorized discharge of approximately 420-558 barrels of 15.1 PPG Zinc Bromide (ZnBr2) into the Gulf of Mexico. The incident was contributed to by the operator's failure to complete the submitted Well Suspension Procedure in a safe and timely manner despite knowing inclement weather was approaching.

The Emergency Disconnect Sequence was initiated, unlatching the Lower Marine Riser Package (LMRP) from the Blow Out Preventers (BOP). This resulted in an unauthorized discharge of approximately 420-558 barrels of 15.1 PPG Zinc Bromide (ZnBr2) into the Gulf of Mexico. The incident was contributed to by the operator's failure to complete the submitted Well Suspension Procedure in a safe and timely manner despite knowing inclement weather was approaching.

incident_summary: On June 27, 2022, at approximately 1755 hours, a fire occurred inside a Pressurized Welding Enclosure (PWE) on the Northeast side of Green Canyon Block 645 Platform A (Holstein). The fire was caused by improperly wrapped Panther felt on Zone 4 deluge wiring, which allowed slag from hot work to smolder inside the wrapping. The fire was discovered when the firewater pump activated and deluge Zone 4 was triggered by the burning wires that controlled the deluge system. Contributing factors included only one Firewatch being present during the 30-minute cool-down period instead of the required two, and fire and smoke detection systems being disabled when the hot work control panel was turned off.

supporting_text: ["The current PRS panel is not equipped with a positive latch indicator that informs the operator that the pipe is completely latched", "The PRS indicator panel displayed that the gripper had been initiated to the closed position"]
incident_summary: On February 14, 2021 at 0330 hours, a stand of 5.5-inch drill pipe fell approximately 4 feet from the Pipe Racking System (PRS) gripper to the rotary table during drilling operations on the Holstein Spar Rig. The PRS indicator panel displayed that the gripper was initiated to the closed position, but the pipe was not actually properly latched. No injuries were reported. The incident was caused by lack of a positive latch indicator on the PRS panel and failure of the rig crew to visually verify that the drill pipe was properly gripped, despite JSA recommendation to do so.

incident_summary: At approximately 2040 hours on 06-SEP-2018, a high gas alarm on the main deck gas compression skid tripped on the Stampede floating production platform (OCS-G 26313, Green Canyon 468-A). Investigation revealed the gas release was caused by four loose fastening bolts on the sight glass of the Flash Gas Compressor 2-A Gas Filter Coalescer (MAJ-1950A). The loose bolts allowed the gasket to cut out under normal operating pressure (~300 psi), resulting in gas release and platform emergency shutdown. No injuries were reported.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Specification of correct seal materials (Viton O-rings)
Role: Prevent installation of incompatible seals
LOD Basis: Material specification control
supporting_text: ["The technician was unaware of a BP policy that only Viton O-rings (Green Tweed 938 elastomer) should be used instead of the Teflon seals", "The correct seals were not installed due to personnel not having knowledge of the importance of the seals"]
incident_summary: On May 8, 2018 at approximately 2115 hours, a high pressure gas release occurred on sales gas meter #3 at the Atlantis platform in Green Canyon Block 787-A. The release was discovered by third party personnel and lasted approximately 30 minutes. The root cause was the installation of incorrect Teflon seals instead of required Viton O-rings (Green Tweed 938 elastomer) during a transmitter replacement 18 days prior. The seal failed at approximately 2100 psi. This was the fourth gas release incident on BP facilities since 2008 and the third on Atlantis, all due to failed Teflon seals. Contributing factors included lack of knowledge by the technician, poor oversight, inadequate training, bypassed gas detectors, insufficient detector placement, and deficient emergency response procedures.

incident_summary: On December 10, 2016, at 05:40 a.m., a flash fire occurred inside the waste oil incinerator tank on the Pacific Drilling Sharav drillship operating for Chevron in Green Canyon block 807. Tubing supports should be installed in such a way as to prevent stress and sheering."]
incident_summary: On 16 July 2019 at 0700 hours, approximately 4.8 barrels of oil leaked from a 3/4" broken tubing connection (Swagelok fitting) on the gas lift bleed ring of the casing valve at Well R-22 on the unmanned GI 22-R platform. The leak was discovered when an operator on nearby GI 22-L observed a sheen north of the platform. The platform had been shut-in since 28 June 2019 for maintenance, but pressure remained on the gas lift line and well casing. High seas from Hurricane Barry (14-16 ft with occasional 21 ft seas) caused well movement that sheared the tubing. Contributing factors included inadequate tubing support, improperly functioning check valves in gas lift mandrels, incorrect installation of a Flow Safety Valve upstream rather than downstream of the tubing connection, and cleanup procedures that exacerbated pollution to a wildlife refuge.

incident_summary: On January 26, 2018, at the GI 43 AP Platform in the Gulf of Mexico, a loss of containment of hydrocarbons and gas releases occurred. Control Room Operators experienced abnormal operating conditions due to sand and sludge buildup and chemical flocking affecting level sensors. Safety devices were placed in bypass to prevent equipment shutdown while operators flushed level bridles. A hole was discovered in the Gas Vent Header Piping at 1:18 am leaking hydrocarbons. The leak was temporarily sealed with duct tape and absorbent pad. Due to communication failures and personnel falling asleep, the platform continued flowing until 6:45 am when a sheen was observed and a field-wide shut-in was initiated. The incident resulted in a 10-mile sheen and discharge from 5 locations on the platform. Root causes included sludge buildup, collapsed internal packing, failed sump pumps, safety device bypass, and failure to use Stop Work Authority.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Pressure Safety Low (PSL) sensor and automatic shutdown
Role: Detection and isolation of leak
LOD Basis: Automatic detection and response system
supporting_text: ["The pipeline pressure sensor detected the leak, shutting in the platform", "a Pressure Safety Low (PSL) trip on the departing 12\" pipeline, KAH-101 segment #8217"]
incident_summary: On 25 August 2018 at 0730 hours, a 3/8-inch hole developed in a 12-inch departing pipeline (KAH-101, segment #8217) at Fieldwood Energy's Grand Isle 76-A platform, discharging approximately 8.35 barrels (352.27 gallons) of hydrocarbons into the Gulf of Mexico. The Pressure Safety Low sensor detected the leak and shut in the platform. Investigation revealed the pipeline had been converted from gas sales/condensate service to bulk oil service without BSEE approval. Contributing factors included improper Pressure Safety High (PSH) set point at 1200 psi (too close to MAOP of 1203 psi) and improper Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) set point at 1323 psi, both failing to provide adequate overpressure protection. The pipeline pumps' Maximum Discharge Pressure of 1800 psi exceeded the pipeline's MAOP, creating overpressure risk.

incident_summary: On 25 August 2018 at 0730 hours, a 3/8-inch hole developed in a 12-inch departing pipeline (KAH-101, segment #8217) at Fieldwood Energy's Grand Isle 76-A platform, discharging approximately 8.35 barrels (352.27 gallons) of hydrocarbons into the Gulf of Mexico. The Pressure Safety Low sensor detected the leak and shut in the platform. Investigation revealed the pipeline had been converted from gas sales/condensate service to bulk oil service without BSEE approval. Contributing factors included improper Pressure Safety High (PSH) set point at 1200 psi (too close to MAOP of 1203 psi) and improper Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) set point at 1323 psi, both failing to provide adequate overpressure protection. The pipeline pumps' Maximum Discharge Pressure of 1800 psi exceeded the pipeline's MAOP, creating overpressure risk.

incident_summary: On 25 April 2025 at approximately 1330 hours, a Louisiana Safety Systems, Inc. Valve Technician was performing maintenance on a control valve located on a 6-inch gas line at GI 47 AP platform. While disassembling the valve, a sudden pressurized gas release occurred, causing the valve bonnet flange assembly (approximately 50 pounds) to strike the technician in the left chest and abdomen area. The technician was knocked backwards onto an elevated scaffolding platform and possibly lost consciousness. The technician sustained a laceration behind the ear and an anterior rib fracture of the 8th left rib. The incident resulted from multiple failures including inadequate communication between the operator and contractor, failure to obtain required work permits and PIC approval, failure to verify zero pressure on the gas line through proper energy isolation procedures, use of unapproved scaffolding without fall protection, and non-adherence to manufacturer recommendations for valve disassembly.

incident_summary: On 06-AUG-2014 at 0200 hours, the main platform generator at Energy XXI's High Island A368 'A' platform shut down due to severed belts. Two contract operators attempted to start the backup diesel generator. incident_summary: On 16 March 2015 at approximately 14:48 hours, a backfire/explosion occurred at Chevron USA's High Island A582 'C' facility during platform startup after monthly Emergency Shut Down testing. Production Operators were bringing the platform back on line when the purge timer on Ambitrol Heater EAL-1510 was observed not counting down. Investigation revealed an inadequate purge of air within the Ambitrol Heater Cabin before ignition, allowing an explosive fuel mixture to accumulate. The air/fuel ratio valve linkage was initially disconnected, and the Programmable Logic Controller allowed excess fuel introduction during ignition. The unit backfired and dislodged the heater cover. No personnel injury, environmental incident, or fire resulted.

Investigation revealed an inadequate purge of air within the Ambitrol Heater Cabin before ignition, allowing an explosive fuel mixture to accumulate. The air/fuel ratio valve linkage was initially disconnected, and the Programmable Logic Controller allowed excess fuel introduction during ignition. The unit backfired and dislodged the heater cover. No personnel injury, environmental incident, or fire resulted.

incident_summary: On September 4, 2023, at approximately 09:15 hours, an injured person (IP) entered the NBK-1070 Heater Treater vessel on platform HIA 379 B without a Supplied Air Respirator (SAR), wearing only an Air Purifying Respirator (APR). The IP was overcome by toxic fumes and passed out face down in the vessel fluids and sludge. A pump operator noticed the IP missing and alerted the cleaning crew supervisor. The supervisor entered without a respirator, was also overcome, and had to exit. A construction supervisor then entered without respiratory protection, extracted the IP, and CPR was administered for approximately 20 minutes before the IP began breathing. The IP was evacuated by helicopter and treated in intensive care for three days. The incident resulted from failure to follow the confined space entry plan, including no tri-gas testing, no hole watch, improper PPE, and no rescue equipment setup.

incident_summary: On May 26, 2022, BSEE inspectors observed a sheen approximately 30 yards by 200 yards in Gulf of Mexico waters originating from Cox Operating's High Island A 582 C facility. Investigation determined hydrocarbons were discharging from the incoming KAQ 2700 high pressure 8-inch wet gas pipeline segment number 13380 at the plus 10 deck level. The source was identified as a one-quarter inch diameter hole in the pipe due to corrosion. An estimated total of 129.145 gallons of oil was discharged during the bleed down process. The pipeline was repaired with a clamp and returned to service on June 17, 2022.

supporting_text: ["Honeywell inspected the piping segment containing the incident gasket twice within the 36 days prior to the incident. Neither inspection identified any issues with the gasket or flange assembly which Honeywell later concluded were likely already damaged by corrosion at the time of both inspections.", "Honeywell also did not use available technologies that could have helped its inspectors or operators detect either corrosion damage to the incident flange assembly or the HF leak prior to the incident. Such technologies included HF atmospheric monitors and acid indicating paint, both of which are strongly recommended by industry guidance documents and by Honeywell itself."]
incident_summary: Three separate hydrogen fluoride incidents occurred at Honeywell Geismar's HFC-245fa unit between October 2021 and June 2024. The first incident (October 21, 2021) involved catastrophic failure of a spiral wound gasket in a flanged piping connection, releasing 39 pounds of anhydrous HF and fatally injuring Field Operator 2 who was exposed to the toxic vapor. The second incident (January 23, 2023) involved catastrophic failure of a reboiler that had thinned due to HF corrosion, releasing over 800 pounds of anhydrous HF and over 1,600 pounds of chlorine gas; no personnel were injured but a complex-wide shelter-in-place was initiated. The third incident (June 7, 2024) involved partial removal of a blind flange from a valve containing pressurized HF during maintenance, releasing less than one pound of HF and seriously injuring a contract worker. All three incidents were preventable through effective implementation of existing safety management systems.

supporting_text: ["Within the first five years of the 245 unit's operation, Honeywell identified corrosion problems with spiral wound gaskets in certain HF services in the unit. incident_summary: Three separate hydrogen fluoride incidents occurred at Honeywell Geismar's HFC-245fa unit between October 2021 and June 2024. The first incident (October 21, 2021) involved catastrophic failure of a spiral wound gasket in a flanged piping connection, releasing 39 pounds of anhydrous HF and fatally injuring Field Operator 2 who was exposed to the toxic vapor. The second incident (January 23, 2023) involved catastrophic failure of a reboiler that had thinned due to HF corrosion, releasing over 800 pounds of anhydrous HF and over 1,600 pounds of chlorine gas; no personnel were injured but a complex-wide shelter-in-place was initiated. The third incident (June 7, 2024) involved partial removal of a blind flange from a valve containing pressurized HF during maintenance, releasing less than one pound of HF and seriously injuring a contract worker. All three incidents were preventable through effective implementation of existing safety management systems.

The depth is not only important to allow passage of zinc and associated debris (dross, little bits of brick and mortar)", "The dimension is shown as 25 5/8 inches ('1 brick'), about 65 mm. This was far less than most, if not all, zinc refineries.", "Many plants increased the dimension to '2 bricks' after the Australian fatality in 1973.", "The Noyelles-Godault accidents were both on columns with 70mm - '1 brick' underflows"]
incident_summary: On 22 July 2010 at 14:20-16:19, a fatal explosion and fire occurred on Column B, a cadmium distillation column at the Horsehead zinc smelter in Monaca, Pennsylvania. The incident resulted from a partial blockage of the column sump that allowed liquid zinc to back-fill up the column, creating a pressure cooker effect. As the column filled with liquid zinc, internal pressure increased, raising the boiling point of zinc from 907°C to 980°C and creating superheated conditions. The column operated outside design parameters with pressure surges as vapour attempted to escape. Eventually, tray walls failed under the increased internal pressure, releasing a surge of superheated zinc vapour and liquid that ignited explosively, destroying the combustion chamber wall and creating a major conflagration in the workplace. The root causes included sump design with restricted clearance (25.625 inches or 65mm), poorly executed column commissioning that allowed formation of sticky zinc oxide dross/liquid zinc emulsion, high and increasing zinc throughput, inadequate sump temperatures during heat-up, and human factors including normalisation of hazardous conditions due to history of column blockages.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Mechanical Integrity Program for Pumps
Role: Prevent pump seal failure through condition monitoring and maintenance
LOD Basis: Formal mechanical integrity procedures required by PSM standards
supporting_text: ["ITC did not have a formal mechanical integrity procedure in place that defined requirements for maintaining the mechanical integrity of Tank 80-8 and its associated equipment, including the Tank 80-8 circulation pump", "A formal mechanical integrity program for pumps in highly hazardous chemical service could have prevented this incident by providing ITC with additional opportunities to identify pump issues prior to the incident", "The mechanical seal on the pump failed on March 17, 2019, allowing butane-enriched naphtha product to release from the pump while it continued to operate"]
incident_summary: On March 17, 2019, a mechanical seal failure on the Tank 80-8 circulation pump resulted in release of approximately 177+ barrels of butane-enriched naphtha product over approximately 30 minutes. The released flammable vapors accumulated undetected in the absence of gas detection systems and ignited at 10:00:46 a.m. The resulting fire spread to 14 adjacent tanks in the First & Second 80's tank farm due to tank spacing, design, and lack of remotely operated isolation valves. The fire burned for three days, destroying 15 tanks and their contents. A containment wall breach released an estimated 470,000–523,000 barrels of hydrocarbon products, foam, and contaminated water into Tucker Bayou, Buffalo Bayou, and the Houston Ship Channel. The incident caused no fatalities or injuries but resulted in significant environmental contamination, community disruption, and over $150 million in property damage.

incident_summary: On 1 April 2015, the SeaDrill West Vela drill ship under contract to BP reported a dark area identified by ROV approximately 400-500 feet from the wellhead at Well 001 (Tiber #3) in Keathley Canyon Block 147. Samples from the seafloor showed gelatinous material resembling filter cake with 2% oil content, consistent with Synthetic Based Drilling Mud used during the 16.5 inch hole section drilling. The incident resulted from a 22 inch casing shoe failure at 8630 ft md/tvd set in a salt formation. From 18-22 January 2015, approximately 500 barrels of 14.4 ppg synthetic oil base mud were discharged into offshore waters during 14 inch intermediate casing running and cementing operations. Total mud losses during casing operations reached approximately 4802 barrels.

incident_summary: At 20:20 hours on 30 August 2014, the Transocean Discoverer Clear Leader drill ship under contract to Chevron was conducting drilling operations at Keathley Canyon Block 829 when a loss of synthetic base mud was observed from a telescopic slip joint packer failure. Approximately 12 barrels of 14.5 ppg SBM (6.12 gallons synthetic oil) was spilled into the Gulf of Mexico but was restricted to the moon pool area. barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Cast iron y-strainer in isobutylene inlet piping
Role: Filtration device to protect charge pump
LOD Basis: Primary containment barrier
supporting_text: ["a fist-sized piece of metal broke away from the body of a 3-inch cast iron y-strainer", "The cause of the isobutylene release was a brittle overload fracture of the cast iron y-strainer driven by internal pressure", "cast iron, a brittle material that existing industry standards and good practice guidance documents either prohibit or warn against using in hazardous applications"]
incident_summary: On April 2, 2019, at 10:51 a.m., a 3-inch cast iron y-strainer in the isobutylene supply piping ruptured due to brittle overload fracture driven by internal pressure from liquid thermal expansion. The rupture released more than 10,000 pounds of flammable isobutylene, creating a vapor cloud. KMCO operators manually closed valves and activated firewater systems to mitigate the release. However, the isobutylene vapor cloud ignited while two operators were still in or near the vapor cloud, fatally injuring one operator (James Mangum) and seriously injuring two others. At least 28 workers total were injured. The explosion and subsequent fires caused substantial facility damage. KMCO filed for bankruptcy in May 2020.

supporting_text: ["The safety instrumented system's flow meter did not detect the low flow to the fired heater, however, because the process material was diverted through piping that was downstream of the flow meter", "The fired heater was equipped with a safety instrumented system (safety interlock) designed to automatically stop the common fuel supply to all burners when process flow to the heater was detected to be lower than the minimum flow requirement"]
incident_summary: On November 19, 2023, at 12:21 a.m., a metal tube ruptured within a fired heater during initial startup of a renewable diesel hydroprocessing unit at Marathon Martinez Renewables facility in Martinez, California. The rupture released over 200,000 pounds of renewable diesel and approximately 2,200 pounds of hydrogen, resulting in a fire that seriously injured one field operator with third-degree burns over 80 percent of his body. The incident was caused by overheating of fired heater tubes due to: (1) a misaligned (open) valve that diverted significant process flow away from the heater, and (2) two of four burners operating with cover plates blocking air inlets, leading to afterburning in the convection section. The field operator was positioned next to the heater during troubleshooting because adequate safe operating limits and alarms were not in place to alert personnel of unsafe conditions, and personnel chose to troubleshoot rather than remotely shut down the heater.

The incident was caused by overheating of fired heater tubes due to: (1) a misaligned (open) valve that diverted significant process flow away from the heater, and (2) two of four burners operating with cover plates blocking air inlets, leading to afterburning in the convection section. The field operator was positioned next to the heater during troubleshooting because adequate safe operating limits and alarms were not in place to alert personnel of unsafe conditions, and personnel chose to troubleshoot rather than remotely shut down the heater.

Role: Prevent hydrogen accumulation in enclosed spaces
LOD Basis: API RP 14F and API RP 500 requirements for battery installation

incident_summary: An explosion occurred on 14 February 2019 at 0115 on the Mississippi Canyon 109 A (Amberjack) Platform. The explosion originated from a 12 Volt battery located inside a Remote Terminal Unit (RTU) Panel used for gas sales measurement and communication. A rechargeable battery had been overcharged due to improper replacement and installation in a non-vented enclosure, causing hydrogen gas accumulation and ignition of electrical components. The panel door was blown off and debris landed 45 feet away. No injuries or pollution occurred. The root cause was flawed equipment design and installation of non-rated electrical components in a hazardous area, compounded by inadequate personnel training on battery hydrogen venting and lack of hazard analysis during design changes.

incident_summary: At approximately 0055 hrs on 1 April 2019, LLOG Exploration Offshore LLC experienced a spill of 61 barrels of Synthetic Oil-Based Mud (SBM) on the Seadrill West Neptune Drillship during drilling operations at Mississippi Canyon Block 199. The spill resulted from a misalignment of trip tank valve positions. While the Derrickman was transferring mud between trip tanks per driller request, the master dump valve for the Trip Tanks was found to be locked in the open position, allowing 61 bbls of SBM to flow into the overboard discharge line into the Gulf of Mexico. The pit cleaning crew had possibly locked open the master dump valve during cleaning operations and never returned it to its normally closed and locked position. Total pollution was 37.8 bbls (61 bbls at 62% synthetic make-up).

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Flame detector in ZAN-700 turbine generator enclosure
Role: Detection of fire initiation
LOD Basis: Early warning system enabling rapid response
supporting_text: ["Flame detectors installed in the ZAN-700 turbine generator enclosure alerted the control room", "the control room received an alarm from a flame detector located inside the ZAN-700 turbine generator enclosure"]
incident_summary: On 30 November 2021 at 1917 hours, a fire occurred in the ZAN-700 turbine generator enclosure at the Appomattox semi-submersible production platform. Flame detectors alerted the control room, and a deck operator confirmed smoke and sparks from the generator housing. The generator ESD was activated, platform ESD initiated, and full muster sounded. Fire teams extinguished the fire with water. Investigation determined that vibration caused loss of fastener preload in the exhaust hatch bolts, allowing hot exhaust to escape and ignite Visqueen (plastic tarp) left by painters, which then melted cables. No injuries, explosions, or environmental harm occurred.

incident_summary: On 15 July 2020 at 1411 hours, the Appomattox platform (MC 437 A) experienced an unplanned gas release while attempting to start up Bulk Gas Compressor #1. Multiple gas detectors alarmed at 2:19 pm, triggering an automatic Process Shut Down. A deck operator detected the odor of gas and the control room operator manually activated the Emergency Shut Down and Muster Alarm. No injuries or damages occurred. The facility safety system responded in accordance with applicable regulations.

incident_summary: On 15 July 2020 at 1411 hours, the Appomattox platform (MC 437 A) experienced an unplanned gas release while attempting to start up Bulk Gas Compressor #1. Multiple gas detectors alarmed at 2:19 pm, triggering an automatic Process Shut Down. A deck operator detected the odor of gas and the control room operator manually activated the Emergency Shut Down and Muster Alarm. Investigation revealed that level bridle drain valves on the BGC #1 Gas Scrubber had been left open. Approximately 1838 kg of gas was released over a 25-minute period. No injuries or damages occurred. The facility safety system responded in accordance with applicable regulations.

incident_summary: On 13 February 2015 at 1115 hours, a 260 barrel discharge of 15 ppg Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SOBM) occurred at MC 762 on the Stena Forth. The Derrickman intended to transfer salt water from active pit #2 to active pit #4 but was unaware that SOBM from equalized active pits #1 and #3 had flowed into active pit #2 due to failure of the air actuator for active pit #2 and the suction valve for active pit #3. Poor communication between the Derrickman and Mud Loggers, combined with equipment failure and lack of continuous pit monitoring, resulted in the unauthorized discharge.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Risk Assessment During Planning and Review Phases
Role: Identify and mitigate risks associated with fluid loss control device shearing
LOD Basis: Procedural development and line-by-line review should identify risks before execution
supporting_text: ["The risk, consequences, and mitigations associated with the inadvertent shearing and opening of the fluid loss control devices were not adequately assessed during the planning and review phases of the completion.", "Within the Kosmos Management System, there are opportunities for this risk to be addressed during the procedural development phase and the line-by-line procedural review, as well as during the Complete Well on Paper (CWOP)."]
incident_summary: On January 9, 2021, at approximately 06:00 hours, Kosmos Energy experienced a well control incident on Seadrill West Neptune during completion operations on the SS003 well. While displacing wellbore completion fluid to packer fluid, the downhole Equivalent Circulating Density (ECD) unintentionally sheared the upper and lower Fluid Loss Control Devices (FLCD), resulting in cross flow between completed intervals and fluid losses of 600 barrels per hour. The well was subsequently shut in and killed over a multi-day operation. The incident was attributed to inadequate risk recognition, poor communication, insufficient execution and supervision, and incomplete procedures.

incident_summary: On 15 September 2015 at 0510 hours, a fire occurred inside Pressurized Welding Enclosure (PWE) #39 at Murphy Exploration MC 736-A platform during hot work operations. Hot slag from torch welding rolled under the Velcro portion of the PWE wall, heated up and ignited. The fire was detected and extinguished by SafeZone personnel using a water fire extinguisher. Contributing factors included improper PWE installation with gaps, lack of readily available fire protection equipment, use of incorrect fire extinguisher type, failure to follow hot work procedures, and delayed communication to control room.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Workover Fluid Cleanup Verification Procedure
Role: Prevent mixing of workover and process fluids
LOD Basis: Primary control to prevent hazardous fluid mixture
supporting_text: ["BP decided to place the well into production before verifying that the workover fluids were cleaned up", "BP placed the MC 822 TD003 S45 subsea well into production before verifying that the workover fluids were cleaned up"]
incident_summary: On 13 July 2020 at 1055 hours, a pollution event occurred at BP's Thunder Horse platform (MC 778-A) in the Gulf of Mexico. While ramping up production following pigging and flowback operations, production operators observed a sheen covering 90% of a 100-yard area. BP's failure to adequately flush workover fluids from subsea well MC 822 TD003 S45 before placing it into production resulted in abnormal emulsion and foaming in process vessels. This caused an estimated 3 barrels of oil to be discharged into the Gulf of Mexico through the overboard produced water handling system. The incident was not reported to the National Response Center or BSEE until 23 July 2020, 10 days after occurrence, violating reporting requirements.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Job Safety Analysis (JSA) Preparation
Role: Administrative control to identify hazards before work
LOD Basis: Pre-job planning and hazard identification
supporting_text: ["the BP production operators prepared a Job Safety Analysis (JSA) to load a pigging device into the oil export pig launcher"]
incident_summary: On 2 August 2020 at 1925 hours, BP production operators were preparing to load a pigging device into the oil export pig launcher KAH-4190 at Thunder Horse platform in the Gulf of Mexico. When operators began cracking open the launcher door, a gas cloud was observed emanating from the top of the launcher. The operators immediately shut the door. The combustible gas detector ASH 962-102, located 3 feet above the launcher, activated and reached 88% of the Low Explosive Limits (LEL). The gas cloud dissipated to 0% LEL within 2 minutes. The incident was caused by oil residue in the pig trap generating a hazardous atmosphere after the launcher had been isolated for 5 days. The launcher was not purged with nitrogen immediately before opening the door, contrary to standard operating procedures. All safety systems responded as designed and no injuries occurred.

barrier_role_match_text:

Role: Prevent excess clarifier chemical from entering produced water discharge
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["During a shut down, the water clarifier injection quill to Float Cell #2 was left open which gravity drained 286 gallons of water clarifier into the Float Cell #2"]
incident_summary: A pollution event occurred on 4 May 2021 at Thunder Horse platform (MC 778 A) when approximately 5 barrels of hydrocarbons were released into the Gulf of Mexico from the produced water outfall. The incident was caused by operator error leaving the water clarifier injection quill to Float Cell #2 open, allowing 286 gallons of water clarifier to gravity drain into the vessel. This excess clarifier caused a lack of oil/water separation. The issue was prolonged because a faulty level control valve in Float Cell #1 prevented switching to the backup produced water train. The sheen was first detected via satellite imagery on 4 May 2021 and the discharge continued through 9 May 2021.

barrier_role_match_text:

incident_summary: On 09 May 2025 at 1530 hours, an Emergency Shutdown event occurred at MC 127 A SPAR platform when EnerMech technicians accidentally initiated a pressure loss in the Temperature Safety Element loop while troubleshooting an air compressor. No injuries occurred, but the firewater system was damaged and unable to provide adequate firefighting capability.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Emulsion breaker chemical trial approval process
Role: Management approval gate for chemical trials
LOD Basis: Pre-incident approval by BSEE
supporting_text: ["Although the chemical trial was approved by BSEE, the trial chemical proved to be non-compatible in the produced water system"]
incident_summary: On 08 February 2025, BP personnel at Na Kika platform began injecting a trial emulsion breaker chemical into the produced water treatment system. The new chemical caused frothing and foaming within production vessels, leading to faulty level readings and liquid carryover. This resulted in approximately 89 barrels of oil and grease discharging from the produced water overboard system into the Gulf of America from 08-15 February 2025. The discharge went undetected for 7 days due to the discharge point being located 80 feet below the water surface. Once BP identified the chemical trial as the cause, they discontinued the trial and returned to the original formula, successfully ending the pollution event.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Temperature Safety Element (TSE) Zone 1A detection system
Role: Early detection of abnormal temperature conditions
LOD Basis: Automatic detection system that triggers ESD
supporting_text: ["A Control Room Operator (CRO) saw on the Human Machine Interface (HMI) screen that the platform had tripped on a Temperature Safety Element (TSE) for Zone 1", "the rental air compressor TSE system (Zone 1A TSE) tripped four seconds after the facility's Generator Fire-Eye Detection system tripped"]
incident_summary: On 24 December 2023 at 0743 hours, a fire occurred on a rental air compressor being used for painting and blasting operations on the FPU 'Who Dat' in MC 547 A, approximately 39 miles offshore in 3280 feet of water. The fire was caused by failure of a 90° elbow on the oil separator piping, which allowed oil to spray into the enclosure and ignite on hot surfaces. The platform's fire detection system and Emergency Shut Down system functioned as designed. The fire was safely extinguished by the platform's Fire Team using water. No injuries, environmental impact, or platform damage occurred. Damage was limited to the rental air compressor and minor charring of adjacent generator enclosure wiring. Cost of repairs/replacement: $31,969.09.

barrier_role_match_text:

Role: Primary barrier to prevent uncontrolled flow
LOD Basis: Primary safety system for subsea well control
supporting_text: ["In attempting to open the Surface-Controlled Subsurface Safety Valve (SCSSV), there was an inability to build pressure in the SCSSV control lines", "The pressure loss was from liquid being released to the environment through the High Pressure (HP) vent on the tree due to the SCSSV control lines loss of integrity"]
incident_summary: On January 6, 2023, at 13:00 hours, Shell Offshore Inc. experienced a pollution event at Mississippi Canyon 943 where subsea well PN003-ST2-BP1 released approximately 16.61 barrels of fluid mixture (hydraulic fluid, methanol, calcium bromide completion brine, and synthetic based oil) to the environment. The leak originated from loss of integrity in the Surface-Controlled Subsurface Safety Valve (SCSSV) control lines, with fluid being released through the High Pressure vent on the subsea tree. The leak was detected and stopped by closing control valves SV1 and SV2 via ROV. The well was secured with 2 barriers and remains shut in with continuous pressure monitoring.

incident_summary: On 10 May 2023 at approximately 0930 hours, two workers employed by Grand Isle Shipyard were removing bolts from a flange on a 2-inch Well Equalization Header line during a scheduled shut-in at MC 941 Platform A (Titan). As the workers loosened the last bolt, trapped pressure within the line escaped and sprayed the two workers in the face with a small amount of hydrocarbon fluid. The incident was caused by oversight of residual fluids and trapped pressure in a dog-leg bend above the flange during the isolation process.

incident_summary: On July 11, 2018, at 1200 hours, an estimated 17,098 gallons of Mono Ethylene Glycol (MEG) was discharged subsea from the Na Kika Platform (MC 474-A) following repair work on PZZ 2591/92 MEG pumps. The leak occurred through leaking Automatic Operated Valve (AOV) and Shut Down Valve (SDV) into the EA2 umbilical circuit. The leak went undetected for approximately 4 days (July 11-15, 2018) due to subsea location and pollutant dissipation before reaching the surface. The incident resulted in an unauthorized discharge violation and failure to notify BSEE.

incident_summary: On 1-Dec-2018 at 03:35 hours, the drillship Pacific Sharav experienced extreme unexpected changes in wind speed and direction due to strong thunderstorms in Mississippi Canyon Block 607, causing a forced vessel excursion. The vessel crossed the Dynamic Positioning Red Alert threshold at 03:38 hours. At 03:39 hours, the Senior Toolpusher manually activated the Emergency Disconnect Sequence (EDS-3), which actuated the Blowout Preventer Casing Shear Rams and sheared the drill pipe. The LMRP was disconnected from the BOP at 03:40 hours, resulting in the release of 1,673 barrels of Synthetic Oil-Based Mud into Gulf of Mexico waters. The well was secured with no indication of flow verified by ROV at 06:18 hours.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Management of Change (MOC) procedures
Role: Administrative control to ensure operational changes are reviewed and procedures updated
LOD Basis: Preventive control to identify need for procedure updates when operational conditions change
supporting_text: ["From the end of 2017 to the time of the incident, water production increased by approximately 6,000 barrels per day (BPD). However, the increase in water production did not prompt a review of the produced water handling system and well start up procedures.", "Management Systems- Inadequate Management of Change (MOC) procedures: The change in water production did not prompt a review of the produced water handling system."]
incident_summary: On May 16, 2018, a pollution event occurred at Noble Energy's Mississippi Canyon 736 A (Thunder Hawk) Platform in the Gulf of Mexico. The incident involved the discharge of approximately 14.77 barrels of oil from the facility's overboard water line. The pollution event was preceded by three separate shut-in and start-up events between May 13-16, 2018. During production ramp-ups, operators pumped methanol and hydrate inhibitor into flowlines, which increased demand on the overboard water treatment system. Level control issues in process vessels, combined with inadequate operational procedures for handling increased water production (6,000 BPD increase since end of 2017), resulted in oily water discharge. The root cause was identified as inadequate Management of Change procedures and inadequate operational procedures for well start-up sequences.

The pollution event was preceded by three separate shut-in and start-up events between May 13-16, 2018. During production ramp-ups, operators pumped methanol and hydrate inhibitor into flowlines, which increased demand on the overboard water treatment system. Level control issues in process vessels, combined with inadequate operational procedures for handling increased water production (6,000 BPD increase since end of 2017), resulted in oily water discharge. The root cause was identified as inadequate Management of Change procedures and inadequate operational procedures for well start-up sequences.

incident_summary: A pollution event occurred on 7 June 2021 at Thunder Horse platform (MC 778 A) when attempting to bring the S5 well online from a long-term shut-in. During well unloading operations using dual Float Cell (FC) operations, the liquid rate exceeded the capacity of FC 1 and FC 2, resulting in produced water issues. incident_summary: On June 2, 2019 at 14:30 hours, the Seadrill West Capricorn Semi-Submersible Drilling Rig encountered a well control event while drilling the Taggert #3 Well in Mississippi Canyon Block 816. The drill crew observed increased return flow with increased gas at the surface (max gas=1386 units). After closing the BOP upper annular and diverter bag, approximately 4 barrels of Synthetic Oil-Based Mud (SBM) were diverted to the Gulf of Mexico waters. No injuries occurred. The probable cause was inadequate mud weight to balance pore pressure in the newly drilled hole section.

barrier_role_match_text:
