supporting_text: ["Cam lever dust caps prevent access to fill lines by capping the end of the fill lines when they are not in use", "The sodium hypochlorite dust cap was unable to be locked because it was missing a split ring on one lever", "Without the split ring on one of the cam levers, it was impossible to secure the dust cap on the fill line with the padlock"]
incident_summary: On October 21, 2016, at approximately 7:35 AM, a Harcros Chemicals cargo tank motor vehicle arrived at MGPI Processing facility in Atchison, Kansas to deliver 30% sulfuric acid. During unloading operations, the driver inadvertently connected the sulfuric acid discharge hose to the unsecured sodium hypochlorite fill line instead of the sulfuric acid fill line. Approximately 4,000 gallons of sulfuric acid mixed with 5,850 gallons of sodium hypochlorite in the bulk tank, initiating a highly exothermic reaction that produced a greenish-yellow toxic gas cloud containing chlorine and other compounds. The cloud migrated northeast from the facility, impacting workers onsite and community members. Over 140 individuals sought medical attention; one MGPI employee and five community members were hospitalized. The incident was mitigated by emergency responders closing the cargo tank discharge valve and activating the facility's deluge system.

incident_summary: Two explosions occurred at Midland Resource Recovery facility in Philippi, West Virginia on May 24, 2017 and June 20, 2017. The May 24 incident killed two workers (including company founder/president) and severely injured another. The June 20 incident fatally injured a contractor from Specialized Professional Services, Inc. Both explosions occurred during draining of Peerless MP odorizers that had been chemically treated with sodium hypochlorite. The probable cause was reactive, unstable chemicals generated during treatment of uncharacterized liquid residues in the vessels. MRR lacked effective process safety management systems to identify and control hazards from reactive chemicals.

barrier_role_match_text:

Role: Prevent rapid depressurization and mixing of hot gas with cold liquid
LOD Basis: Designed to automatically execute proper defrost sequence including bleed cycle

This resulted in an interruption of a defrost cycle that was in progress", "the control system did not recognize that the blast freezer evaporator unit contained high-pressure hot gas when it allowed the suction stop valve to open", "This manual bypass of the programmed defrost sequence allowed the low-temperature liquid and hot gas to mix in the same pipe"]
incident_summary: On August 23, 2010, a hydraulic shock event caused catastrophic failure of a 12-inch roof-mounted suction pipe at Millard Refrigerated Services, releasing 32,100 pounds of anhydrous ammonia. The incident was triggered by a 7-hour power outage followed by manual interruption of a defrost cycle, which bypassed the programmed bleed sequence. When the system restarted, high-pressure hot gas mixed with low-temperature liquid in the suction line, causing condensation-induced shock and vapor-propelled liquid phenomena that ruptured both the roof piping and an evaporator coil. The ammonia cloud traveled 0.25 miles across Theodore Industrial Canal, exposing 153 people including 1 Millard employee, 9 ship crew members, and 143 offsite contractors. Of those exposed, 32 were hospitalized and 4 placed in intensive care.

incident_summary: On September 22, 2019, at 06:55 hours, an Electric Line Operator (ELO) at McMoRan's Main Pass 299 A facility sustained puncture wounds to his right-hand fingers, left forearm, and left torso when a Green Detonator blasting cap exploded during continuity testing. The incident resulted in the ELO being transported by helicopter for medical evaluation, which determined that only first aid was required.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Wet seal system with labyrinth seals
Role: Prevent gas from entering lube oil system
LOD Basis: Primary barrier to maintain separation between gas and lube oil

incident_summary: On 21 September 2021 at approximately 1550 hours, an Emergency Shut Down occurred at MP 42 D/L/M facility. A leak in the ESD/TSE loop caused loss of main power and rendered both primary and secondary seal oil pumps inoperable. Combined with a liquid carryover event in the first stage suction scrubber, produced gas bypassed the wet seals and entered the lube oil tank. The overpressurized tank lid blew off, spraying lube oil and natural gas onto the hot turbine engine, causing ignition and rapid fire spread. All personnel were safely evacuated with no injuries or pollution.

Role: Prevent pressurized gas release
LOD Basis: N/A

incident_summary: On July 4, 2014 at 1300 hours, construction workers on the main deck heard an unusual noise from the cellar deck. Natural gas was observed leaking from the upstream flange of the Flow Safety Valve (FSV) on the FA-2 flow line segment of the B-001 well. The leak was caused by multiple valve deficiencies: the Manual Master Valve, Surface Safety Valve, and Wing Valve were all leaking, allowing shut-in tubing pressure of 3200 PSIG to reach the out-of-service production header, causing gasket failure on the FSV upstream flange. These valve deficiencies had been previously identified on August 16, 2013 but were not mitigated by management. The facility was evacuated and the well was isolated.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Process Knowledge Management System
Role: Ensure comprehensive understanding and documentation of chemical hazards and decomposition risks
LOD Basis: Industry best practice (CCPS guidance)
supporting_text: ["Clearon lacked effective process knowledge management systems, and as a result the Technology Package that Clearon delivered to Optima Belle as part of the tolling arrangement did not adequately communicate the circumstances and temperatures that could lead to the hazardous decomposition of CDB-56\u00ae", "inadequate literature searches for publicly available or internal Clearon-owned NaDCC dihydrate data", "The CSB does not have evidence that a comprehensive evaluation was conducted to identify the potential hazards of using an alternate dryer"]
incident_summary: On December 8, 2020, at approximately 10:00 p.m., a pressure-rated rotary double cone dryer containing sodium dichloroisocyanurate dihydrate (NaDCC dihydrate, CDB-56®) exploded at Optima Belle LLC in Belle, West Virginia. The explosion occurred during a toll manufacturing operation in which Optima Belle was dehydrating CDB-56® to produce anhydrous sodium dichloroisocyanurate (CDB-63®) on behalf of Clearon Corporation through a contractual agreement with tolling outsourcing partner Richman Chemical Inc. (RCI). The chlorinated isocyanurate compound underwent an unexpected self-accelerating decomposition reaction, releasing gases that increased the dryer internal pressure above its design pressure, causing the dryer to explode. Metal debris and dryer fragments propelled off-site and within the facility, striking a methanol pipe that subsequently caught fire. One Optima Belle employee (John Mark Gillenwater II) was fatally injured, two others were evaluated for respiratory irritation, and one Kanawha County resident reported a minor leg injury. Debris was found almost a half mile from the site. Estimated property damage was $33.1 million.

incident_summary: On December 8, 2020, at approximately 10:00 p.m., a pressure-rated rotary double cone dryer containing sodium dichloroisocyanurate dihydrate (NaDCC dihydrate, CDB-56®) exploded at Optima Belle LLC in Belle, West Virginia. The explosion occurred during a toll manufacturing operation in which Optima Belle was dehydrating CDB-56® to produce anhydrous sodium dichloroisocyanurate (CDB-63®) on behalf of Clearon Corporation through a contractual agreement with tolling outsourcing partner Richman Chemical Inc. (RCI). The chlorinated isocyanurate compound underwent an unexpected self-accelerating decomposition reaction, releasing gases that increased the dryer internal pressure above its design pressure, causing the dryer to explode. Metal debris and dryer fragments propelled off-site and within the facility, striking a methanol pipe that subsequently caught fire. One Optima Belle employee (John Mark Gillenwater II) was fatally injured, two others were evaluated for respiratory irritation, and one Kanawha County resident reported a minor leg injury. Debris was found almost a half mile from the site. Estimated property damage was $33.1 million.

incident_summary: On February 8, 2017, at approximately 11:05 am CDT, a foul condensate tank at the PCA DeRidder containerboard mill exploded during annual maintenance outage. The tank, part of a non-condensable gas system, contained water with a layer of flammable turpentine and an explosive vapor space. Air entered the tank through a pressure vacuum breaker during normal pressure swings and cooling, mixing with turpentine vapor to create an explosive atmosphere. Hot work (welding) on water piping above the tank likely ignited the explosive mixture. The explosion killed three contractors and injured seven others, launching the 100,000-gallon tank approximately 375 feet over a six-story building. The incident resulted from inadequate process hazard analysis, ineffective safeguards, and failure to apply process safety management systems to the non-condensable gas system.

incident_summary: On 30 June 2022 at 2346 hrs, while conducting subsea pipeline modification operations on PSN 11015 in the Gulf of Mexico (205 ft water depth, 41 miles offshore), a diamond wire saw cut through an 18-inch diameter pipeline. At approximately 65% completion of the cut, the pipeline suddenly separated due to unknown stored energy, causing each end to move in opposite directions. This unexpected torsion/tension caused the temporary 8-inch bypass piping to shear at a flange connection, releasing approximately 90 barrels of crude oil into the Gulf of Mexico over a 3-day period. The incident was caused by personnel not being aware of the amount of stored energy within the pipeline, and was contributed to by lack of communication with a pipeline contributor prior to performing the cut.

incident_summary: On June 21, 2019 at 4:00:16 a.m., a steel pipe elbow in the HF alkylation unit ruptured after the control room operator increased feed to the T-7 propane stripper. The elbow, containing high concentrations of nickel and copper, had corroded from HF exposure and thinned to 0.011 inch (less than 7% of retirement thickness). The rupture released approximately 95% propane, 2.5% HF, and other hydrocarbons, forming a large vapor cloud. The cloud ignited two minutes later at 4:02:06 a.m. The RAD system was successfully activated, draining 339,000 pounds of HF. However, the water mitigation system control communication failed at ignition, and backup power failed nine seconds later, preventing remote activation of water spray cannons. Three secondary explosions occurred, with the largest at 4:22 a.m. when vessel V-1 ruptured, ejecting a 38,000-pound fragment across the Schuylkill River. Manual activation of the water pump at 4:39 a.m. allowed HF mitigation spraying. The fire was extinguished on June 22. Total HF release was estimated at 5,239 pounds, with 3,271 pounds released to atmosphere. Approximately 676,000 pounds of hydrocarbons released, 608,000 pounds combusted. Property damage estimated at $750 million, ranking as the third-largest refinery loss worldwide since 1974. Five workers and one firefighter sustained minor injuries. No off-site fatalities reported.

LOD Basis: OSHA PSM and EPA RMP regulations requiring compliance with RAGAGEP
supporting_text: ["The seminal research presented in the 2003 NACE paper 03651 Specification for Carbon Steel Materials for Hydrofluoric Acid Alkylation Units directly led to changes in industry guidance quantifying the levels of nickel and copper in steel that could be considered safe for use in HF alkylation units", "Prior to the incident, API RP 751, Sunoco, and PES did not effectively respond to these advancements in industry knowledge by ensuring the safety of existing facilities through requiring all carbon steel piping circuit components to be inspected"]
incident_summary: On June 21, 2019 at 4:00:16 a.m., a steel pipe elbow in the HF alkylation unit ruptured after the control room operator increased feed to the T-7 propane stripper. The elbow, containing high concentrations of nickel and copper, had corroded from HF exposure and thinned to 0.011 inch (less than 7% of retirement thickness). The rupture released approximately 95% propane, 2.5% HF, and other hydrocarbons, forming a large vapor cloud. The cloud ignited two minutes later at 4:02:06 a.m. The RAD system was successfully activated, draining 339,000 pounds of HF. However, the water mitigation system control communication failed at ignition, and backup power failed nine seconds later, preventing remote activation of water spray cannons. Three secondary explosions occurred, with the largest at 4:22 a.m. when vessel V-1 ruptured, ejecting a 38,000-pound fragment across the Schuylkill River. Manual activation of the water pump at 4:39 a.m. allowed HF mitigation spraying. The fire was extinguished on June 22. Total HF release was estimated at 5,239 pounds, with 3,271 pounds released to atmosphere. Approximately 676,000 pounds of hydrocarbons released, 608,000 pounds combusted. Property damage estimated at $750 million, ranking as the third-largest refinery loss worldwide since 1974. Five workers and one firefighter sustained minor injuries. No off-site fatalities reported.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Burner Safety Low alarm system
Role: Detection of unsafe burner conditions
LOD Basis: Alarm detection of low flame condition
supporting_text: ["On 9/23/2021, the Burner Safety Low (BSL) alarm for EAW-2910 and EAW-2920 went off. Two operators went to investigate."]
incident_summary: On September 23, 2021 at 1010 hours, a Burner Safety Low alarm occurred on the NBK-2900 Heater Treater at Talos Energy's South Pelto Platform. Two operators responded: one at the burner location and one at the master panel. After resetting the fuel shutdown valve relay and relighting the burners, a pressure release occurred when the West side burner was lit. The root cause was identified as restricted airflow in the stack arrestor from a previous fire tube fire on August 1, 2021, combined with insufficient purge time of residual gas before reignition. A second incident occurred on September 30, 2021 during restart, revealing charred wiring on the igniter and ground wires. The burner section was permanently taken out of service.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Production alarm system
Role: Detection of abnormal conditions
LOD Basis: Alarm detection of burner safety low condition
supporting_text: ["a production alarm sounded at 1930 hours", "Personnel went to the panel to investigate and found the burner safety low (BSL) indicator for the Heater Treater (NBK-2900) activated"]
incident_summary: On 29-FEB-2016 at 1930 hours, a fire occurred inside the 24-inch fire tube of the Heater Treater (NBK-2900) on the South Pelto PL 23-D platform. The fire was caused by thermo-galvanic corrosion of the A-106 Grade B schedule 20 fire tube material. Multiple corrosion-accelerating parameters were present: low pH produced water (2.95), high chloride concentration (25,028 mg/l), temperature gradient on the metal surface, and scale deposition. The phosphoric acid in the emulsion breaker contributed to the low pH. The calculated pitting corrosion rate was 450 mils/year, an extremely high rate requiring thermo-galvanic corrosion as the driving force. The fire was quickly extinguished with no injuries or evacuation. The platform remained shut in for repairs until 18-MAR-2016.

incident_summary: At approximately 1730 hours on 25-June-2018, an H2S release occurred on the South Pelto 5-B platform during abandonment operations involving flushing of eight platforms and fifteen pipelines. A worker detected an odor of H2S at an open top flow back tank. An H2S detector recorded 100 ppm of gas. All operations were stopped and personnel mustered for safety. H2S readings steadily decreased from 100 ppm to 0 ppm over nine hours. No personnel were injured. Investigation determined that water left in pipelines for over two years prior to abandonment operations allowed high concentrations of bacteria growth, which led to small pockets of H2S gas formation.

barrier_role_match_text:

Role: Primary barrier to prevent gas influx
LOD Basis: Mud density and column height maintained above formation pressure
supporting_text: ["Underbalanced drilling was performed without needed planning, equipment, skills, or procedures, thus nullifying the planned primary barrier to prevent gas influx", "Tripping was performed out of the underbalanced well, which allowed a large amount of gas to enter the well", "A weighted pill intended to overbalance the well was apparently miscalculated. After pill placement, the well was still underbalanced"]
incident_summary: On January 22, 2018, a blowout and rig fire occurred at Pryor Trust Well 1H-9 in Pittsburg County, Oklahoma. The fire killed five workers inside the driller's cabin from thermal burn injuries and smoke/soot inhalation. The blowout occurred approximately 3.5 hours after tripping drill pipe out of the well. Root causes included failure of the primary barrier (hydrostatic pressure from drilling mud) and secondary barrier (human detection of influx and BOP activation). Contributing factors included underbalanced operations performed without proper planning or equipment, inadequate training on electronic trip sheets, equipment misalignment causing data interpretation errors, failure to identify surface pressure, miscalculated weighted pill, deactivated alarm system, lack of flow checks, inadequate driller training in influx detection, and ineffective safety management system. The BOP failed to close after the fire because hydraulic control hoses burned and leaked, depleting accumulator pressure.

incident_summary: On 8 May 2022 at 1130 hours, a sheen of unknown origin was observed on the water at Mississippi Canyon Block 940, OCS-G 31534. The leak originated from Subsea Well MC 940 (Vito) VA007, a completed well not yet tied back via pipeline to Shell's Vito facility. The source was identified as a tubing string parting at a coupling in the 5.5" tubing at approximately 5,122 feet below mudline. The leak was stopped within 4 days by closing the SV2 needle valve on the subsea tree. An estimated 7 barrels of oil were released into the Gulf of Mexico between 8 May and 12 May 2022. Shell's response and reporting were found to be in compliance with federal regulations.

barrier_role_match_text:

incident_summary: On November 4, 2009, a sudden and complete rupture occurred in the 10-inch diameter discharge line at the exit of the South Reactor (R30101) in the Mobil Distillate Dewaxing Unit (MDDW) during hot hydrogen catalyst regeneration. The rupture released approximately 175.3 kg of hydrogen-rich gas mixture at 642 psig and 785°F. The released gas ignited approximately 0.66 seconds after rupture, causing an explosion that damaged residential homes in the adjacent community. Metallurgical analysis revealed wall thinning from the inside diameter through the weld metal and base metal of the 10-inch straight segment, consistent with sulfidation corrosion. The piping had been in service since 1993 with no documented wall thickness monitoring between installation and the incident.

incident_summary: On December 3, 2014, a sheen was reported in the vicinity of South Marsh Island Area Block 27. Pipeline Segment Number 3334 operated by Sea Robin Pipeline Company was identified as the source. A hole measuring 1/2" by 3/4" was located at the 6 o'clock position at the bottom of the line at 93 feet water depth. The spill volume was initially reported as 15 barrels but later revised to 0.7 barrels of condensate. The leak was secured on December 5, 2014, and repairs were completed by December 12, 2014. Investigation findings indicated no corrosion, no third-party damage, and normal cathodic protection readings, suggesting the probable cause was a manufacturing defect or isolated pit in the pipe.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Compressor piston and rod maintenance inspection
Role: Detect early signs of component degradation
LOD Basis: Preventive maintenance to identify scarring and wear before failure
supporting_text: ["scarring was found on the piston. As per the compressor mechanic, the scarring that was discovered is generally a sign of poor maintenance"]
incident_summary: On January 16, 2024, at 0252 hours, the C1 compressor at Arena Offshore's SM-128B facility experienced a piston failure. The piston rod nut loosened due to component fault or wear, causing the two bottom connecting rod bolts to break. This allowed the piston rod nut to contact the head end of the cylinder and separate from the frame. As the housing broke, gas ignited. The vibration switch detected the abnormal vibration and tripped, but failed to initiate shutdown at the panel due to moisture in the output line that froze in the 17°F ambient temperature. The night operator discovered the fire, activated the fire alarm and ESD, alerted personnel, and the fire was extinguished without injuries.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Protective coating on pipeline flange
Role: Prevent external corrosion initiation

incident_summary: On December 15, 2023, at 0520 hours, an 8-inch bi-directional gas pipeline (Segment 5114) containing 1220 psi ruptured on the Talos ERT LLC SM 130 B facility in the Gulf of Mexico. The rupture resulted in a fire that traveled approximately 80 feet from below the +10 level to the production deck. A 3-foot section of pipeline from the +4 to +7 elevation was missing. The fire lasted 5-7 minutes before gas pressure bled down. Life saving equipment, wiring, and minor structural damage occurred near the +10 level. No injuries were reported. Favorable wind conditions (28 mph blowing away from platform) prevented more severe damage. Root cause was determined to be severe external corrosion of the pipeline flange driven by high chlorine concentrations (12,000 ppm) in an oxygen-containing environment, combined with inadequate maintenance of protective coating and failure to complete required mechanical integrity inspection.

Root cause was determined to be severe external corrosion of the pipeline flange driven by high chlorine concentrations (12,000 ppm) in an oxygen-containing environment, combined with inadequate maintenance of protective coating and failure to complete required mechanical integrity inspection.

The pressure safety valve (PSV) was forcibly dislodged due to improper sizing and poor threading alignment, releasing liquids into the atmosphere. incident_summary: On March 7, 2025, at 08:15 hours, a flash fire occurred at W&T Offshore's production facility at Ship Shoal Block 80, Platform A while attempting to bring Pipeline Pump #2 (PAX-360) online. A W&T Mechanic and Contractor Electrician sustained burn injuries to head and hands. The fire resulted from a loose fitting on the fuel gas starter exhaust piping that allowed gas to accumulate near the pump. When the engine backfired during startup, the accumulated gas ignited. Both injured personnel were evacuated and treated; the Electrician was released to return to work on April 9, 2025, while the Mechanic continued treatment. The incident was exacerbated by personnel not wearing required PPE (gloves) and wind direction that pushed flames toward one worker.

incident_summary: On October 11, 2017, at 0015 hours, a fire occurred on top of Gas Compressor No. 1 on the South Timbalier 28 PRD Platform. The fire was caused by a failure/rupture of the third stage cooler tube resulting in a gas release, with static charge as the probable ignition source. The fire was extinguished immediately and the compressor was secured.

incident_summary: On May 17, 2014, at 0830 hours, while performing abandonment operations on Walter Oil & Gas's #001 well on the Rowan Gorilla IV, three employees were burned by a flash fire. The crew was welding pad eyes to the inside of a 36-inch casing to enable removal. Burning slag fell inside the wellbore, igniting gas that had migrated from the reservoir after the casing was cut. The three employees suffered first and second degree burns to their upper bodies. Investigation revealed gas was present at approximately 40 feet depth inside the casing but not at the surface where employees were working.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Ball valve self-locking mechanism
Role: Prevent inadvertent opening of drain valve
LOD Basis: Primary mechanical control to prevent valve opening
supporting_text: ["operator inadvertently opened a ball valve connected to the bottom of the LSH bridle with his foot/shin while exiting the area", "It is possible the self-locking mechanism on the ball valve handle was stuck in the 'up' position at the time the incident occurred"]
incident_summary: At approximately 1200 hours on January 23, 2020, at South Timbalier Block 311 Platform A, an operator inadvertently opened a ball valve connected to the bottom of an in-service LP Gas Compressor 3rd stage scrubber LSH with his foot/shin while exiting the area. This released approximately 1100 psi through a 3/8" stainless steel tubing drain line approximately 7 feet in length. The pressure release caused a whipping action in the drain tubing striking the operator on the side of the face/hardhat. Hydrocarbons from the 3rd stage suction scrubber vented toward the in-service LP gas compressor engine and ignited. Personnel activated the manual ESD station, signaled the fire alarm, and all personnel mustered. An attempt to use a nearby 125 lb. wheeled fire extinguisher unit failed to discharge. Fire water was used to extinguish the fire. Personnel reported first aid injuries (scraps, bumps, and skin redness). The fire caused damage to the compressor engine electrical components/wiring and the 3rd stage discharge PSV.

incident_summary: On September 3, 2018, approximately 517 barrels of synthetic based mud (SBM) were inadvertently discharged into the Gulf of Mexico during a fluid transfer operation from the work boat 'Esplanade' to the H&P 100 platform rig. The discharge was caused by a gasket failure in a 4-inch butterfly valve combined with the absence of a secondary TODO Dry Break valve that had been removed but not reinstalled. The night crew failed to reinstall the TODO valve despite communication from the Deck Foreman that it needed to be in place prior to fluid transfer.

incident_summary: On August 12, 2016, at approximately 8:15 PM CDT, a flash fire and explosion occurred while L-Con contractor was welding a piping segment at the Sunoco Nederland crude oil terminal. The 30-inch diameter piping section contained residual crude oil and was isolated using CARBER isolation tools. During internal welding of flange A, vapor inside the piping ignited, causing a pressure spike that ejected the isolation tools from both ends of the pipe. The explosion and flash fire injured seven contractors (four welders, one fire watch, two others), with four requiring hospitalization for burns, bone fractures, and blast-related injuries. The incident was caused by hot work being conducted on equipment containing an explosive atmosphere without proper clearing or inerting of flammable material.

incident_summary: On April 2, 2010, the Tesoro Anacortes Refinery experienced a catastrophic rupture of heat exchanger E-6600E in the Naphtha Hydrotreater (NHT) unit. The carbon steel heat exchanger failed due to High Temperature Hydrogen Attack (HTHA), releasing highly flammable hydrogen and naphtha at temperatures exceeding 500°F. The release ignited, causing an explosion and fire that burned for over three hours. Seven Tesoro employees (one shift supervisor and six operators) working in the immediate vicinity were fatally injured. This was the largest fatal incident at a US petroleum refinery since the BP Texas City accident in March 2005.

incident_summary: On February 12, 2014, two Tesoro Martinez refinery employees suffered first and second degree chemical burns while working to return a sulfuric acid sampling system to service following nonroutine maintenance. Concentrated sulfuric acid sprayed the workers following a mechanical integrity failure of a ¾-inch tubing connector adjacent to the acid sampling station. The tubing came apart at the connector when operators opened a valve to return the acid sampling system to service. Acid continued to spray from a nearly 100,000-gallon acid settler for over two hours, resulting in the release of approximately 84,000 pounds of sulfuric acid. Both workers were transported to a burn center and neither returned to work for over 150 days. The incident was caused by insufficient tightening of the compression joint. The CSB investigation found that weak process safety culture at the refinery, including failure to implement inherently safer design, inadequate PPE availability, poor maintenance practices, and failure to learn from previous incidents, were causal to the incident.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Procedure to minimize popcorn polymer hazards associated with dead legs
Role: Prevent accumulation of popcorn polymer in dead legs

supporting_text: ["CSB determined that CSB did not have an effective safety management system in place to identify when the safety-critical procedure could not be conducted as intended, or to identify the associated safety implications when the procedure could not be implemented"]
incident_summary: A dangerous substance known as popcorn polymer accumulated in a temporary dead leg created when a process pump was taken out of service for maintenance at the Huntsman Chemical Plant in Port Neches, Texas. The popcorn polymer expanded and ruptured the piping, releasing butadiene. The butadiene release resulted in fires that burned for more than a month, causing $40 million in on-site property damage and $9 million in off-site property damage. Two CSB employees and a contractor reported minor injuries, and at least five local residents reported injuries. The incident led to a mandatory four-mile radius evacuation order affecting Port Neches, Groves, Nederland, and portions of Port Arthur.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Hazard analysis for parts with cavities
Role: Identify accumulation hazards before processing
LOD Basis: Process safety management system
supporting_text: ["TS USA did not have an adequate process safety management system in place at its Chattanooga facility", "TS USA lacked hazard analyses on processing new parts, on the liquid nitriding line, and for reprocessing parts", "Due to the configuration of the cavity and the small size of the drain hole, the rollers still presented an accumulation hazard. This hazard was not recognized by TS USA's management systems and reviewers"]
incident_summary: On May 30, 2024, at 8:58 a.m., an explosion occurred at the TS USA liquid nitriding facility in Chattanooga, Tennessee, while employees were processing metal rollers with cavities. Water became trapped in a roller cavity due to a solidified salt plug blocking the drain hole. When the roller was reintroduced into the 800°F oxidizing salt bath, the trapped water boiled and rapidly expanded as steam, creating overpressure that ejected the roller's press fit end. The contact of water with the molten salt caused a steam explosion that erupted approximately 4,500 pounds of molten sodium hydroxide and sodium nitrate from the bath, engulfing a line operator and causing second- and third-degree chemical and thermal burns over 95 percent of his body. The operator died later that day at the hospital. Three other employees suffered minor burns. The eruption ignited multiple fires throughout the facility, resulting in approximately $1.3 million in property damage.

incident_summary: On November 21, 2020 at 1945 hrs, a gas leak occurred on the A-Ram Powell platform in Viosca Knoll Block 956. The second hatch had one latch-bolt that was not secured to the hatch, and the other four latch-bolts were also loose", "The O-rings on the hatches were not maintained, as they were absent of grease, showed visible cracks, and signs of deterioration"]
incident_summary: On August 11, 2024, at approximately 7:10 p.m., an explosion and fires occurred on the deck of the liftboat Brazos at Vermilion Block 170 A during a well cleanup and test operation. Two Production Technology & Services Inc. (PTS) workers sustained second-degree burns and required evacuation by USCG. The incident resulted from improperly latched hatches on a 500-barrel tank allowing gas to escape, which accumulated near a rental diesel generator. The generator's faulty electronic control module failed to shut down the engine when it overran due to gas ingestion, causing ignition of the gas plume. Contributing factors included lack of written procedures, inadequate hazard analysis, poor maintenance of tank seals, and failure of the generator's automatic emergency shutdown system.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Fuel system gasket integrity and maintenance
Role: Prevent fuel gas leak from cylinder head fuel injection line
LOD Basis: Primary engineering barrier to prevent gas release
supporting_text: ["The No. 6 left bank cylinder head fuel injection line had gas leaking due to a failed gasket where the machined flange bolts to the cylinder head", "The findings indicated that the cause of the gas leak was a cut gasket on the fuel valve jumper line"]
incident_summary: On 29-NOV-2020 at 0657 hours, a flash fire occurred during maintenance troubleshooting of a gas compressor engine (CBA-2030) at the West Delta Block 105 Platform E. A mechanic was troubleshooting a non-firing No. 6 left bank power cylinder. When the mechanic disconnected the secondary spark plug wire from the ignition coil, a spark was introduced into an area containing leaked fuel gas at explosive concentrations. The gas ignited, causing a flash fire. The root cause was a cut gasket on the fuel valve jumper line that allowed gas to leak. The mechanic immediately activated the ESD system, descended the catwalk, and extinguished the fire with a fire extinguisher. No injuries, pollution, or property damage occurred. The platform was shut in until repairs were completed and permission to restart was granted by BSEE on 01-DEC-2020. The probable cause was identified as inadequate hazard analysis in the Job Safety Analysis (JSA), failure to use a gas detector, and failure to utilize a hot work permit.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Pipeline Burial/Cover Protection
Role: Physical barrier to prevent trawl net contact with pipeline infrastructure
LOD Basis: Regulatory requirement 30 CFR 250.1003(a)(2) mandates at least 3 feet of cover for pipeline tie-ins and similar obstructive items
supporting_text: ["Neither the application nor the completion report contains any indication that the spool was buried or covered", "30 CFR 250.1003(a)(2) states that pipeline valves, taps, tie-ins, capped lines, and repaired sections that could be obstructive shall be provided with at least 3 feet of cover", "The BSEE GOMR Pipeline Section does not contain any approved departures for this pipeline segment"]
incident_summary: On September 9, 2018, at 1130 hours, a trawling vessel snagged a bi-directional pipeline (Segment No. 15815) in the Gulf of Mexico, causing it to rupture and release approximately 0.8 gallons of hydrocarbon. The pipeline failed due to excessive stresses imposed when the trawler's net snagged on an 8-inch to 6-inch tie-in spool and dragged it until failure. The tie-in spool, installed in 2007 as part of a Right-of-Way modification, was not sufficiently buried or covered as required by regulations. The incident resulted in an estimated $2,225,000 in property damage.

incident_summary: On 09 January 2021 at approximately 0600 hours, a pin hole leak developed in the inlet suction piping of the gas compressor on West Delta 30 Platform P. The leak activated the Pressure Safety Low (PSL) system, which automatically shut down the gas compressor, LP separator, and all nine LP production wells. The leak was caused by excessive corrosion resulting from Cox's failure to maintain protective coatings on the process piping. No loss of containment or pollution was observed. A temporary clamp was installed with BSEE approval and the platform was returned to production within 4 hours.

incident_summary: On 09 January 2021 at approximately 0600 hours, a pin hole leak developed in the inlet suction piping of the gas compressor on West Delta 30 Platform P. The leak activated the Pressure Safety Low (PSL) system, which automatically shut down the gas compressor, LP separator, and all nine LP production wells. The leak was caused by excessive corrosion resulting from Cox's failure to maintain protective coatings on the process piping. No loss of containment or pollution was observed. A temporary clamp was installed with BSEE approval and the platform was returned to production within 4 hours.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Wet Oil Transfer Pump (PBA-410E)
Role: Prevent oil accumulation in floatation cell oil chamber by transferring oil to storage
LOD Basis: Primary mechanical control to prevent oil level rise
supporting_text: ["The Wet Oil Transfer Pump (PBA-410E) required to transfer oil from the Floatation Cell's (ABM-410) oil chamber, failed to pump due to a stuck, internal pump shifting/slider valve."]
incident_summary: On 21 September 2016 at 0828 hours, BSEE Inspectors discovered an oil sheen 7 miles long and 1 mile wide originating from WD 32 E Platform. Investigation revealed that the Wet Oil Transfer Pump (PBA-410E) failed due to a stuck internal pump shifting/slider valve, preventing oil transfer from the Floatation Cell (ABM-410). Oil level in the oil chamber rose to the level of the produced water side and flowed back into the overboard discharge chamber. The Fisher 2500 Level Controller maintained preset water discharge level, causing oil to discharge through the overboard water outlet. The Level Safety High (LSH 1) shut down device was not installed in the oil chamber as required, preventing automatic platform shut-in. Approximately 8.05 barrels of crude oil were discharged into Gulf waters. The unmanned facility was in-service and producing at the time of discovery.

incident_summary: On 17 February 2023 at approximately 0315 hours, a gas leak was discovered on a 12-inch gas pipeline riser (PSN 759), approximately 5-7 feet above the water line on the WD 73AT platform. The platform's ESD was activated, shutting down the facility and sounding the alarm. All 26 personnel on board were accounted for and sheltered in place in the quarters building upwind from the leak. The pipeline was bled down over several hours. A light sheen was observed during overflight, later determined to originate from the slop oil tank rather than the pipeline. Investigation revealed inadequate preventative maintenance, failed repairs documented in a 2015 survey, and insufficient pipeline inspection methods.

incident_summary: On April 21, 2025, at 19:30 CDT, the WD 90 B platform experienced a loss of well control during Plug and Abandonment operations on well B020. An uncontrolled release of approximately 20 barrels of well fluids occurred due to a leak at the starter head spool. The incident was triggered when the P&A crew pumped fluid down the tubing to assist pressure equalization across a downhole plug. Oil was observed leaking from between the starter head flange and the surface casing annulus. After approximately 5-10 minutes, the fluid flow reduced and stopped, but uncontrolled gas was then observed, prompting full platform evacuation. All 22 personnel were safely evacuated. The well was shut in by closing the crown valve and cutting the wireline. No injuries were reported.

incident_summary: On January 29, 2020, at approximately 3:00 p.m., the Daniel H. Wendland 1-H well in Burleson County, Texas experienced a loss of well control during a workover operation to replace the tubing head. The well blowout released oil and gas which ignited within seconds, resulting in a flash fire that fatally injured three contract workers and seriously injured a fourth. The incident resulted in an estimated $1 million in property damage. Root causes included inadequate well planning, ineffective well control barriers for underpressured reservoirs, and poor ignition source management.

The well blowout released oil and gas which ignited within seconds, resulting in a flash fire that fatally injured three contract workers and seriously injured a fourth. The incident resulted in an estimated $1 million in property damage. Root causes included inadequate well planning, ineffective well control barriers for underpressured reservoirs, and poor ignition source management.

incident_summary: On April 17, 2013, at approximately 7:51 pm CDT, a fire and explosion occurred at the West Fertilizer Company facility in West, Texas. The facility stored between 40 and 60 tons of fertilizer grade ammonium nitrate (FGAN). The explosion fatally injured 12 emergency responders and 3 members of the public, with more than 260 injured. The blast completely destroyed the WFC facility and caused widespread damage to over 150 offsite buildings, including schools, a nursing home, and residential structures. The total insurance-related losses were estimated at approximately $230 million.

supporting_text: ["First, the MPD system was put into Flow Control mode", "the Surface Back Pressure (SBP) in the MPD system increased to over 1,300 psi exceeding the 1,300 psi PRV setting", "when the PRV's set point is exceeded the MPD system reverts to 'Manual Mode'"]
incident_summary: On November 16, 2022, at 05:11 AM, a saltwater kick occurred during drilling of the 12-1/4 inch hole section at approximately 25,000 feet on the Diamond Offshore West Vela drillship. The kick caused Surface Back Pressure in the MPD system to exceed the 1,300 psi PRV setting. Approximately twelve barrels of Synthetic Based Mud were discharged overboard via the MPD system Pressure Relief Valves. The well was successfully shut in and the kick was circulated out. The incident was attributed to human performance error in the decision to divert drilling fluid overboard rather than to the flowline as specified in the New Technology Application.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Lock-out/Tag-out (LOTO) procedures and documentation
Role: Administrative control to track valve key custody and prevent unauthorized valve operation
LOD Basis: Primary administrative barrier to prevent loss of control during equipment transitions
supporting_text: ["the keys for Pit #2's overboard dump valves were issued to the on-tour Derrickman by the Assistant Rig Manager, but no entry was made into the rig's Lock-out/Tag-out log book", "During the transition, the keys for Pit #2's dump valves were passed on again to the oncoming Derrickman. Again, no entries were entered into the rig's Lock-out/Tag-out log book"]
incident_summary: On December 11, 2014, the Noble Jim Day unintentionally discharged 142 barrels of Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SBM) into the Gulf of Mexico during mud pit cleaning operations. The discharge resulted from valve misalignment during the transition between crew changes. Keys to the dump valves were passed between personnel without proper Lock-out/Tag-out documentation. The Derrickman, lacking knowledge of proper valve alignment and receiving poor communication and labeling, misaligned the valves and created a pathway for hydrocarbons to discharge overboard. The Pit Volume Totalizer alarm detected the loss after approximately 5 minutes, at which point the crew secured the pits and corrected the valve alignment.

incident_summary: On October 27, 2014, while drilling at 15,902 feet in a salt zone, the Maersk Valiant experienced a gas influx resulting in approximately 55 barrels of mud being discharged onto the rig floor. The well was shut-in using the Upper Annular, but the shut-in pressure caused fracturing of the formation and 22 inch casing shoe. A second influx of 16 barrels occurred on November 21, 2014, which was contained with no surface release. No injuries or environmental pollution resulted from either incident.

barrier_role_match_text:

Role: Automatic detection and response to loss of packer seal pressure

incident_summary: While performing drilling operations on the Diamond Ocean Blackhawk for Anadarko Petroleum Corporation, two incidents occurred in which Synthetic Based Mud was inadvertently discharged into the Gulf of Mexico. The first incident on July 4, 2015 released 2.5 barrels of SBM, and the second on August 8, 2015 released 3.3 barrels of SBM. Both incidents were caused by a malfunction in the rig's software for the Slip Joint Packer management system, combined with inadequate crew monitoring of packer pressures. The software logic automatically adjusted low alarm set points based on instrument air pressure fluctuations without crew knowledge, causing the packer system to vent mud when pressure dropped below the adjusted set point.

incident_summary: On 26-NOV-2016 at approximately 1406 hours, a high pressure gas release occurred around the gas export metering skid on the FPSO Turritella at Walker Ridge Block 551-A. During an attempt to collect gas samples, an engineer mistakenly pulled a half-inch plug from a double block and bleed valve without first bleeding down the line, despite system pressure of up to 2500 psi. The escaping gas was detected by the gas detection system, triggering an automatic facility shutdown. The engineer sustained a minor injury and received onboard medical treatment. No evacuation was necessary.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Pressure vessel design and construction standards
Role: Prevent inadequate vessel design
LOD Basis: Design phase control to ensure vessel can withstand operating pressures
supporting_text: ["Kettle 3 normally operated at or near atmospheric pressure but was known to potentially build pressure up to 15 pounds per square inch gauge (psig) during some upset conditions", "After the new manway was added, Yenkin-Majestic performed a leak check up to 4 psig and allowed Kettle 3 to continue operating for approximately three months until the incident", "On the day of the incident, Kettle 3's new manway did not withstand more than 9 psig of pressure during a process upset and leaked hot resin and flammable solvent vapor into the facility", "Yenkin-Majestic did not follow basic quality assurance practices, such as adequately pressure testing equipment after alterations"]
incident_summary: On April 8, 2021, at approximately 12:02 a.m., a mixture of flammable naphtha solvent vapors and resin liquid became pressurized and released through the seal of a closed manway on Kettle 3 at the Yenkin-Majestic OPC Polymers resin plant in Columbus, Ohio. The release occurred after the kettle operator turned on the agitator, which caused rapid vaporization of solvent that had been added to the hot resin while the agitator was unexpectedly off. The flammable vapor cloud spread throughout the enclosed building and formed both inside and outside. Approximately two minutes later, at 12:04 a.m., the vapor cloud ignited, causing an explosion and large fire. One employee was fatally injured from thermal injuries and inhalation of combustion products. Eight other employees were transported to hospitals with injuries including third-degree burns and limb fractures, with one requiring leg amputation. The incident caused over $90 million in property damage.

The release occurred after the kettle operator turned on the agitator, which caused rapid vaporization of solvent that had been added to the hot resin while the agitator was unexpectedly off. The flammable vapor cloud spread throughout the enclosed building and formed both inside and outside. Approximately two minutes later, at 12:04 a.m., the vapor cloud ignited, causing an explosion and large fire. One employee was fatally injured from thermal injuries and inhalation of combustion products. Eight other employees were transported to hospitals with injuries including third-degree burns and limb fractures, with one requiring leg amputation. The incident caused over $90 million in property damage.

supporting_text: ["Yenkin-Majestic allowed resin plant operators to wear cotton short-sleeved shirts while working in proximity to flammable materials unless they were performing specific tasks", "Had the resin plant employees been required to wear flame-resistant personal protective equipment (PPE), multiple employee burn injuries resulting from the explosion and fire may have been reduced or prevented", "Four of the injured employees were burned by the fire, two of whom received third-degree burns"]
